Added لِ at beginning to show Noah is speaking to his people

steps.m: to stand together, rise up as one

steps.n: Changed the verb into a noun meaning 'people' - those who stand together as one group
Added kasra at the end to show this word is being possessed by something
Added يٰ at the beginning to show calling out to someone
This is a conditional particle that introduces the condition 'if it was difficult for you'. It connects to the verb كَانَ that follows it.

steps.m: to be or exist in a state

steps.n: Added short vowels (fat-ha) to make it past tense meaning 'he was'

steps.m: he was/existed (describing the state before إِن)
steps.n: The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas. This is a common change in Arabic for words with و in the middle.
The damma (ُ) in the middle shows something naturally grows or becomes bigger over time
This is a preposition derived from the concept of being above or elevated
The ى changes to يْ to connect with the next part. This is like a bridge between parts of the word.
Added كُم at the end to show this affects multiple people being spoken to. This appears in the context where Nuh is speaking to his people.
Changes from verb (to stand) into noun meaning place where standing happens
Adds genitive case ending to show possession or after certain prepositions

steps.m: my place of standing (in this ayah: referring to Nuh's position among his people)
steps.n: Added ى at the end to show it belongs to the speaker (Nuh)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Changed to a noun showing the action of reminding
Added و to connect this word to what came before it
Added kasra at the end to connect it to the next word showing ownership
Added ى to show this is my reminding. This connects to Allah (اللَّهِ) in the next part of the ayah.

steps.m: a sign (something that shows or proves)
steps.n: Root letters were reshaped to make a noun meaning 'one sign/proof' that shows something
Changed from one sign to many signs by adding ات
Added بِ at the beginning to show 'with/by' and ِ at the end because it's connected to Allah (اللَّهِ) in the phrase 'signs of Allah'
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word in Arabic that always refers to The One True God.
Added kasra at the end to show this is the object of تَوَكَّلْتُ (I relied upon). Also added shadda on ل and changed initial alif to wasl form since it connects with previous word.
This is a preposition showing something is on top of or depending on something else
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous statement. This connects to the verb تَوَكَّلْتُ (I rely/depend) that comes after it.
Changed to the proper name for Allah by adding ال and merging letters
Changed to show 'of Allah' - the word gets kasra (i) at the end when it's owned by something. Also has shadda mark on ل and special alif at start

steps.m: to trust or depend on someone

steps.n: Added vowels and doubled the middle letter to show intensive meaning
Added تَ at beginning to show doing the action to/for oneself
Changed from 'he put trust' to 'you put trust!' - making it a command
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did this action in the past. This word shows Nuh putting his complete trust in Allah.

steps.m: to gather or bring things/people together

steps.n: Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make a basic past tense verb

steps.m: he caused to gather/he brought together
steps.n: Adding أ at start changes meaning to 'make something happen'
Adding وا at end makes it a command for a group of people
Added فَ (meaning 'so' or 'then') to connect this command with what came before it in the verse
The word already has all required elements - no further changes needed

steps.m: to command/order, to deal with matters

steps.n: The root letters are connected with vowels to create a noun meaning the actual matter or affair being dealt with. This word is what is being gathered/decided in the command فَأَجْمِعُوٓا۟ (so gather/decide).
Added كُمْ at the end to show this matter belongs to all the people being spoken to

steps.m: to partner with, to associate with

steps.n: Changed the word to mean multiple partners by adding ُ on first letter, ا after second letter, and اء at the end (like changing 'partner' to 'partners' in English)
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this to the command 'gather' (فَأَجْمِعُوٓا۟), showing what should be gathered
Added َ (fatha) at the end to show this is the object - the partners that are being gathered
Added possessive pronoun suffix meaning 'your (plural)'

steps.m: then (connects this command to the previous one)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that shows one thing happens after another. In this ayah, it connects two commands together, showing what should happen next.
This is a particle that negates the verb that comes after it (يَكُنْ). Together they mean 'let not be' or 'should not be'.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: let it be/may it be (jussive form after لا)
steps.n: The word changes because it comes after لا (not). The long vowel و gets dropped and the word gets a sukun at the end, making it shorter. This makes it mean 'let it not be' instead of just 'he is'.
Adding vowels creates a noun meaning the thing being commanded or decided. In this ayah, it's what the people are being told to gather/decide about.
The damma (u sound) at the end shows this word is the subject/doer in the sentence
Adding كُمْ shows this belongs to all the people being spoken to
Basic preposition meaning 'on' or 'upon'
When adding a pronoun after على, the ى changes to يْ to connect with what follows
Added كُمْ at the end to show this applies to multiple people being spoken to

steps.m: to cover or cloud over (like when something blocks your view)

steps.n: The root letters were squeezed together and a damma was added to show something was done to make it unclear (like when clouds block your view)
Added ة at the end to make it into a noun describing the state

steps.m: a source of confusion (object of يَكُنْ)
steps.n: Added tanween to show this is the object being affected in the sentence. It's what should not 'be' (يَكُنْ) for them.
This is a connecting word that shows one thing happens after another, with some time passing in between. It's different from فَ which shows things happening right away.
Basic command form
Adding vowels to make it a command form
Added plural command ending وا with Quranic marks (small alif ٓ and small circle ۟). Changed kasra to damma before وا.
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or destination. Since it's a particle, we start with its basic form rather than a root.
Added the shadda and fatha (َّ) at the end to show this means 'me'. This connects the preposition to who it's pointing to.
This is a negative particle that makes the following verb negative, like adding 'not' in English. In this ayah, it negates the verb تُنظِرُونِ (give respite)
The و (waaw) at the start connects this phrase to the previous command (ٱقْضُوٓا۟ - decide), making it part of the sequence of actions
Connected letters with fatha vowels to make past tense verb
Added تُ prefix to change from 'he' to 'you', and changed vowels to make it present tense instead of past
Added ون to show multiple people are doing the action

steps.m: you (all) give me time (in context: don't wait for me)
steps.n: Added نِ at the end to mean 'me' - showing who receives the action (I am the one being given time)
This is a conditional particle that shows uncertainty about something happening. It's like saying 'if' in English when you're not sure if something will happen.
The فَ prefix connects this condition to what came before it

steps.m: to turn away or take charge

steps.n: Added vowels to make it mean 'he turned away' in the past tense
Doubled the middle letter to show completeness of action
Added تَ to show doing action to oneself
Changed final ى to يْ to prepare for suffix
Added تُمْ at the end to show 'you all' did the action in the past
This is a question word used to ask about something. Here it's asking about the reward mentioned in the next part of the ayah.

steps.m: then what (connecting to فَإِن تَوَلَّيْتُمْ - 'if you turn away')
steps.n: The فَ shows this question follows from the previous statement 'if you turn away.' It's like saying 'If you turn away, then what reward have I asked from you?'
Added basic vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed from 'he' to 'I' by adding تُ at the end
Added كُم to show who was being asked. This word connects to مِّنْ أَجْرٍ (for reward) in the ayah
This shows where something comes from or what it's made of. Here it connects 'reward' (أَجْرٍ) to 'I asked you' (سَأَلْتُكُم)
The shadda (ّ) is added to make it easier to say after the previous word. It's like when we say 'an apple' instead of 'a apple' in English - it just sounds better
Basic noun form showing the thing itself rather than the action
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) to show this is any general reward. This comes after مِنْ (from/of) in the phrase مِّنْ أَجْرٍ meaning 'of any reward'.

steps.m: if (introduces condition for سَأَلْتُكُم - I asked you)
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces a hypothetical situation. In this ayah, it connects the condition 'if you turn away' with its result about not asking for payment.

steps.m: to pay, reward, or give wages

steps.n: Connected the root letters together and added vowel marks to make it a noun meaning the thing given as payment
The kasra is added to the second root letter to prepare for the possessive construction
Added ي at the end to show this reward belongs to me, like saying "my reward"
This is a basic particle that shows something is being excluded. It's a complete word that doesn't come from a root.
The shadda and alif are added to complete the word's standard form. In this ayah, it excludes what comes after it (عَلَى ٱللَّهِ - 'upon Allah') from what came before (أَجْرِىَ - 'my reward').
This is a preposition that shows something is on top of, dependent on, or relying upon something else. Here it connects to the word اللَّهِ (Allah) to show that the reward/payment depends on Allah.
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word in Arabic that always refers to the One True God.
Added kasra at the end to show possession or connection, and shadda on ل to emphasize it. The hamza at the start changed to a special connecting hamza (ٱ).
Changed the vowels to show that someone received the command rather than gave it

steps.m: I was commanded (subject of أَنْ أَكُونَ)
steps.n: Added تُ at the end to show 'I' am the one who received the command. This word connects to أَنْ أَكُونَ (that I be) in the ayah, showing what was commanded.

steps.m: and I was commanded (connected to فَمَا سَأَلْتُكُم)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) at the beginning to connect this statement to the previous phrase فَمَا سَأَلْتُكُم (I did not ask you). The full meaning is: 'I did not ask you [for reward]... and I was commanded...'
This particle introduces what someone should, will, or wants to do. Here it connects to the verb أَكُونَ (I be) to show what was commanded.
Added basic vowels to connect the letters
Added أَ at the beginning to show 'I' am doing the action in the present tense
Added fatha (َ) at the end to show this is in the subjunctive mood
Basic particle that shows something comes from or is part of something else
Added fatha at the end to help connect smoothly to the next word (الْمُسْلِمِينَ). This is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to submit peacefully, to surrender willingly

steps.n: The مُ at the beginning and ِ in the middle work together to show someone who does the action of submitting peacefully
The ين at the end shows we're talking about many people who submit

steps.m: the ones who submit peacefully (مِنَ shows this word follows 'from/among')
steps.n: ٱلْ at the start makes it specific, and َ at the end shows it follows مِنَ (from/among) in the ayah
Added vowels to connect the letters into a past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter shows doing something with intensity or repeatedly
Added وا at the end to change from 'he denied' to 'they denied'
Added فَ to show this happened as a result of something before it
Added هُ to show who they denied (referring to the messenger mentioned before)
Doubling the middle letter shows doing the action with intensity or repeatedly
Adding نا indicates first person plural past tense. This is a complete verb form, not in a case relationship with other words.
Adding هُ shows who was saved - in this case 'him'
Conjunction prefix adds sequential meaning
This is a question/relative pronoun word that means 'who' or 'whoever'. It's used to ask about or refer to people.

steps.m: and who (connected to فَنَجَّيْنٰهُ - so We saved him)
steps.n: Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this phrase to the previous one. This shows that after Allah saved him (Noah), He also saved those who were with him.
Added basic vowel marks to show pronunciation
Added shadda for emphasis and to connect with previous word
Added هُ (hu) at the end to mean 'him' - refers back to Noah who was mentioned earlier in the story
Added the small و mark (ۥ) after هُ to show proper pronunciation
This is a preposition that shows Noah and his companions were inside the ship
Added kasra (ِ) under ف to show how to pronounce it as 'fee'
Changed the ي to ى which is just a writing style change - it means the same thing

steps.m: ship (because it moves in circular paths through water)
steps.n: The ُ and ْ pattern creates a noun for something related to the circular motion - in this case a ship that moves in paths through water
Adding ٱلْ makes it refer to a specific ship rather than any ship

steps.m: in the ship (connected to فِى before it)
steps.n: The ِ at the end shows this word follows فِى (in) from earlier in the verse
Connected the root letters with fathas to make the basic past tense verb
Added و at the beginning to connect this action with what came before it
Added نا at the end to show 'we' did the action instead of 'he'

steps.m: and we made them (into خَلٰٓئِفَ - successors)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show who the action was done to. This word connects to خَلٰٓئِفَ which tells what they were made into - successors.

steps.m: to follow or take someone's place

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters and show this happened in the past
Added ي between second and third root letters and ة at the end to show someone who does this action
Changed the word to show many successors instead of just one by using a special plural pattern

steps.m: to sink or submerge in water

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters together and show this happened in the past
Adding أ at the beginning changes the meaning from doing something to making something happen to someone/something else
Adding نا at the end shows that 'we' did this action
Adding و at the beginning connects this action to what happened before it in the story
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to what they describe, like saying 'the one who' in English
Added ال with shadda to make it more specific, like saying 'the one who' instead of just 'who'

steps.m: those who/which/that (plural, refers to those who denied Our signs)
steps.n: Changed from 'the one who' (singular) to 'those who' (plural) by adding ن at the end, to talk about multiple people
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Adding shadda (doubling mark) on middle letter makes the action more intense or repetitive

steps.m: they denied intensely/repeatedly (the signs/verses mentioned in بِـَٔايٰتِنَا)
steps.n: Adding وا changes the subject from 'he' to 'they' (plural). This word connects to بِـَٔايٰتِنَا (our signs/verses) which comes after it in the ayah.

steps.m: to show, make clear, be evident

steps.n: Forms a noun meaning 'sign' from the basic meaning of showing/making clear
Makes the word plural to show multiple signs
Adds نَا to show these are Allah's signs being mentioned
Adds بِ to show these signs were the means by which they denied the truth. This connects to the verb كَذَّبُوا (they denied) in the ayah.

steps.m: to look at or observe carefully

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed from 'he looked' to 'look!' by removing first vowel and changing middle vowel to 'u' sound - this makes it a command
Added helper alif at start to make it easier to pronounce the command form
Added فَ at the beginning to show this command follows from what happened before

steps.m: how (asking about the state of عٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُنذَرِينَ)
steps.n: This is a question word used to ask about the way something happens or the state of something. In this ayah, it's asking about how the end result was for those who were warned.
Adding the vowel marks helps show this is a question word asking 'how'
Added vowels to connect the letters and show past tense. This is the theoretical base form before the weak letter change.

steps.m: he/it was (the outcome of المنذرين - those who were warned)
steps.n: The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas. This is a common change in Arabic to make words easier to pronounce. This word describes what happened to those who were warned.
Changes from basic root to feminine noun meaning 'outcome'
Adds feminine marker to make it refer to an outcome/result
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start and changed vowels to make it passive - now means 'the one who receives the warning' instead of 'the one who warns'
Added ين to make it plural, referring to multiple people who receive the warning

steps.m: the ones who are warned (those who received warning)
steps.n: Added ال to specify these particular warned people and َ at the end because it's the object of the verb أَغْرَقْنَا (we drowned)

steps.m: then (connects this part to what came before)
steps.n: This word connects different parts of the story, showing that sending messengers happened after previous events. It's like saying 'and then' when telling a story.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added نا at the end to show 'we' did the action. This word comes after ثُمَّ (then) in the ayah, showing this sending happened after something else.

steps.m: from (connects بَعَثْنَا 'We sent' to بَعْدِهِ 'after him')
steps.n: This is a helper word that shows where something starts from or comes from. Here it helps show the time sequence - We sent messengers starting from after him.
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to create the basic noun meaning 'after'
Added kasra to show this word is possessed by something coming after it
Added هِۦ to show who/what comes after. The word follows مِنۢ (from) in the ayah.
Root meaning 'send' becomes plural noun 'messengers' by adding u-vowels (dummas) between letters
Added ًا (tanween) to show this is a direct object - the messengers are receiving the action of being sent
This is a preposition that shows direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects 'messengers' (رُسُلًا) with 'their people' (قَوْمِهِمْ), showing the messengers were sent to their people.
Added an alif symbol (ىٰ) at the end to show proper pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to stand together as a group

steps.n: Root meaning 'stand/rise' became 'people/nation', with kasra showing it belongs to something after it

steps.m: their people (referring to the messengers' people)
steps.n: Added هِمْ at the end to show these are the people who belong to the messengers mentioned before (رُسُلًا)
This is the basic concept of coming or arriving
Changed root letters ج-ي-أ into past tense verb جَاءَ by making the middle letter ي into a long ا
Added an extra alif to emphasize the action of coming
Added و and ُ to show multiple people doing the action
Added هُم to show who they came to
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happened as a result of something before it
Added shadda to strengthen the meaning of clarity
Added feminine plural ending to show multiple clear proofs
Added definite article to specify these particular proofs

steps.m: with the clear proofs (object of جَآءُوهُم - they came to them)
steps.n: Added 'with' (بِ) at start, changed spelling for Quran style - this word means 'clear proofs' and is connected to 'they came to them' in the verse
This is the basic negative particle مَا which negates what comes after it

steps.m: so not (negates what comes after it in the ayah)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous statement. In this ayah, it connects to the previous statement about messengers bringing clear signs, leading to 'so they were not...'
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter). This is a common change in Arabic verbs with و in the middle.
Added وا at the end to change 'he was' to 'they were'. This word is connected to فَمَا (then not) in the ayah, showing what they were not doing.
Adding long alif at start changes meaning from 'believe' to 'make someone believe' - like turning 'trust' into 'cause to trust'
Changed from past tense (he believed) to present tense (he believes). The 'yu' at the start shows it's present tense.
Added لِ to show purpose - this explains why something else happens
Added وا to show multiple people doing the action (in this ayah, referring to the people who rejected their messengers)
Basic preposition that shows how something is done
Added مَا which means 'what' to create a compound word. In this ayah, it refers to what they denied before, connecting to كَذَّبُوا۟ (they denied) in the next part.

steps.m: he denied intensively (subject of فَمَا كَانُوا۟)
steps.n: Added shadda (ّ) on middle letter to show strong or repeated denial. This word is connected to فَمَا كَانُوا۟ (then they were not) in the ayah.

steps.m: they denied intensively (subject of فَمَا كَانُوا۟)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show many people (they) did the action, not just one person (he)
Combined the preposition بِ (with/by) with the pronoun هِ (it/him) to show what is being affected

steps.m: with it/by it (referring to what they denied before)
steps.n: Added a small alif (ۦ) after هِ to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
This is a basic preposition showing where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects 'messengers' (رُسُلًا) with 'after him' (بَعْدِهِ), showing when these messengers were sent.
Adding short vowels (fat-ha and sukoon) to the root letters to make it a usable word meaning 'before'
Adding damma at the end shows this word is in the nominative case
This is the basic demonstrative word meaning 'this/that'
The ل is added to show distance, and the final ك makes it a complete demonstrative. The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show it's pronounced longer.

steps.m: like that (referring to what was mentioned before)
steps.n: The ك at the beginning means 'like' or 'similar to', making the whole word mean 'like that' or 'likewise'
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Changed from 'he' to 'we' and from past to present tense by adding ن at the start. Shows Allah speaking about sealing the hearts (قُلُوبِ).
This is the basic preposition showing something is above or has authority over something else. In this ayah, it connects with قُلُوبِ (hearts) to show what is being sealed.
The small alif (ٰ) is added to show that the ى is pronounced as a long 'aa' sound. This doesn't change the meaning, it just helps with pronunciation.
The root meaning of turning becomes 'heart' because the heart is always turning and changing. This is the basic singular form.
Changed the vowels and added و to make the word plural, showing we're talking about multiple hearts.
Added kasra at the end because this word follows على (upon) in the ayah, showing these are the hearts belonging to the transgressors.

Adds مُ at the start to show who does the action, ت between first two letters to show doing something to oneself, and ِ on the middle letter to complete the pattern. Together these changes turn 'crossing limits' into 'one who crosses limits'
Adds ين at the end to show we're talking about more than one transgressor
Adds ال at the start to point to specific transgressors that were already talked about, not just any transgressors

steps.m: the transgressors (object of نَطْبَعُ - we seal)
steps.n: Adds َ at the end because these transgressors are having something done to them - they are having their hearts sealed in this verse
Basic form of this connecting word that shows sequence of events
Added a damma (ُ) mark to show this is pronounced 'thumma' meaning 'then' or 'thereafter'

steps.m: then, next, afterwards (connecting to بَعَثْنَا - We sent)
steps.n: Added shadda (ّ) to make the meaning stronger. This word connects to the next verb بَعَثْنَا (We sent), showing these events happened one after another.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added نا at the end to change from 'he' to 'we'
Basic preposition showing starting point or origin. In this ayah, it connects to the word بَعْدِهِم (after them) to show the sequence of events.
Added a small ن mark (called tanwin) to help with pronunciation when connecting to the next word
Added fatha and sukun to form the basic noun meaning 'after'
Added kasra at the end because something will possess this word
Added هِم at the end to show this means 'after them'
This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew (מֹשֶׁה/Moshe). The long alif at the end (ىٰ) is written with a special mark called an alif maqsura.
The shadda (ّ) is added because this word comes after the word بَعْدِهِم in the ayah, making the م sound stronger when read together.
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object (receiver) of the verb بَعَثْنَا (We sent)
Added و at start to connect this with مُّوسَىٰ (Moses) who was also sent
Basic preposition showing direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects Musa and Harun (موسى وهارون) to Pharaoh (فرعون), showing they were sent to him.
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end which is just a writing variation - it doesn't change the meaning
This is the basic form of the word, which is a proper noun title used for ancient Egyptian kings
Added fatha at the end to show Pharaoh is being acted upon. In this ayah, Moses and Aaron were sent 'to Pharaoh'
Adding the short 'a' vowels (fatha) turns the root letters into 'he filled/gathered'

steps.m: and assembly, and group of nobles
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect this word to what came before it
Added a small line under the last letter (kasra) to show this word connects to 'to' before it

steps.m: and his chiefs (connected to إلى فرعون - to Pharaoh)
steps.n: Added ي and هِ at the end to show possession - this word is part of the phrase 'to Pharaoh and his chiefs'

steps.m: to show, appear, or be clear

steps.n: Root meaning 'to show' became 'sign' by changing middle ي to ا and adding ة to make it a noun
Makes the word plural to show multiple signs
Adds نَا at the end to show these are our signs
Adds بِ at the beginning to show these signs were used as tools or means. This relates to how Moses and Aaron were sent to Pharaoh with these signs.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Form X shows someone actively seeking to make themselves great/important
The وا shows many people did this action

steps.m: and so they made themselves act great (arrogantly)
steps.n: Adds coordinating conjunction to connect with previous statement
Added fathas to make past tense. The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a special type of word (called a hollow verb).
وا indicates third person masculine plural in past tense
Adds conjunction meaning 'and'
Changed the verb into a noun meaning 'people' - the idea comes from people standing together as a group

steps.m: a people (object of كَانُوا - they were)
steps.n: Added ًا to show this word is being acted upon by the verb كَانُوا (they were). Like saying 'they were a people' in English.

steps.m: to do wrong or commit crimes

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding مُ at the start and changing the vowels turns this into a noun for someone who does the action
Adding ين makes the word plural and shows it's describing the word قَوْمًا (people)
Doubling the م adds emphasis to show they are very criminal
This is a basic time particle meaning 'when'. It's used to connect two events where the second event happens right after the first.

steps.m: Then when (followed by جَآءَهُمُ - it came to them)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens after something else. In this ayah, it connects to what happened before with the meaning 'then when it came to them'.
Added fathas to make past tense. The middle letter ي becomes ا because it's a weak letter. This word shows who did the action in قَالُوٓا۟ (they said).
Added long alif to emphasize the action of coming/arriving
Added هُمُ at the end to show who was affected by the action (them)
Adding fatha vowels to create basic past tense verb form
The verb 'to be true' becomes the noun 'truth' by dropping a letter and adding shadda
Adding damma at the end shows this is the subject of the sentence
Adding ال makes it specific - 'the truth' instead of just 'truth'. This word is what 'came to them' in the ayah.

steps.m: from (connects عِندِنَا to what comes before it)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows where something comes from or starts. In this ayah, it connects 'from our presence' (عِندِنَا) to what came before it.
Added vowel marks to make it a preposition. The kasra and sukun pattern is common for prepositions.

steps.m: with, at, by (ready to connect to something)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to prepare for connection with the next part
Added نَا meaning 'us/our' to show who the preposition relates to. This word follows مِنْ (from) in the ayah.
Added vowels (a) to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he said'
The middle letter و changed to ا and وا was added at the end to make it mean 'they' instead of 'he'
This is a special word that emphasizes what comes after it. The shadda (ّ) makes the ن sound stronger, which helps show the strong emphasis.
Basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something nearby
Added ه at the beginning to draw attention to what's being pointed to, like saying 'look at this'
Added the alif with overbar (ٰ) and fatha marks to complete the word. In this ayah, it's pointing to what they're calling magic (سِحْرٌ).
Changed the root into a noun showing the result of doing magic. This word is what إِنَّ (indeed) is describing in this ayah.

Added لَ at beginning for emphasis (meaning 'surely/indeed'), while ٌ at the end shows this is the subject being described. Together with إِنَّ from the ayah, this creates strong emphasis - 'Indeed this is surely magic!'
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past-tense verb
Added مُ at start and changed vowels to show something that actively makes things clear
Added shadda to emphasize the strength of the clarity
Added tanween to show this is a general description
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense. This word is connected to أَتَقُولُونَ (do you say) later in the ayah, showing a conversation.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in Arabic verbs with و in the middle

steps.m: Moses (the subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: This is a proper noun borrowed from Hebrew/Egyptian meaning 'drawn out [of water]', referring to how Moses was found in the river as a baby. In this ayah, Moses is speaking to the magicians.
Added fathas to make the past tense form. This word relates to مُوسَىٰٓ (Moses) who is doing the speaking.
Changed from past tense 'said' to present tense 'saying' by changing middle vowels from 'aa' to 'uu'
Added ون to show multiple male people doing the action
Added 'you' marker at start to change from 'they' to 'you'
Added question marker to turn statement into question
Added plural ending to show multiple people are being addressed. In this ayah, Moses is speaking to multiple magicians.

steps.m: do you (plural) say? (Moses questioning the magicians)
steps.n: Added question marker to show Moses is challenging or questioning what the magicians are saying about the truth when it has come to them.
Root letters combined with shadda (double letter) to make the word 'truth'
Adding definite article ال makes the noun definite
Adding لِ (li) at the beginning means 'for' or 'to' - like saying 'to the truth'
The kasra (ِ) under the last letter shows this word follows the preposition لِ (to/for), and the shadda (ّ) shows doubling of the letter
This is a special word that connects two events in time, showing that one thing happened right after another. In this ayah, it connects when the truth came to them with what Musa said about it.

steps.m: when (connecting to جَآءَكُمْ - it came to you)
steps.n: The shadda (ّ) shows emphasis by doubling the letter م. This makes the connection between events stronger, showing they happened right after each other.
The root letters were connected with fathas and the middle letter ي became ا to make the past tense 'he came'
Added كم at the end to show the object 'you all' (masculine plural)
The note is incorrect - كُمْ is present in both words. The change is the elongated alif (آ) which adds emphasis, meaning 'definitely/surely came'

steps.m: to do magic, to cast spells

steps.n: Changed from 'doing magic' into 'magic itself' by adding vowels to make it a noun

steps.m: Is it magic? (Musa asking about what the magicians called the truth)
steps.n: Added أَ at the beginning to turn 'magic' into the question 'Is it magic?' This is Musa speaking to the magicians who called the truth magic when it came to them.
This is the basic attention-drawing part that starts many pointing words
The alif is replaced with a small alif above the ه to show it's connected to what comes next
Adding ذَا makes it point to something nearby, like saying 'this one here'
This is the basic negative particle that makes the following verb يُفْلِحُ (succeed) negative, changing it to 'not succeed'
Adding وَ connects this with Musa's previous statement, joining his question about magic with his statement that magicians will not succeed
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels. This word is negated by لَا (not) in the ayah, so it means 'does not succeed'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed the word pattern to show who does the action
Added ون to show there are many magicians
Added ال at the beginning to mean 'the' and َ at the end to complete the word. The س gets doubled (shadda) after ال.
Added fathas and changed و to ا because it's a hollow verb (has و in middle). This makes it past tense.
Added وا at the end to show multiple people (they) did the action
Added optional marks (elongation sign ٓ and small و ۟) that don't change the meaning
Root letters ج-ي-أ changed to جَاءَ by adding vowels and turning ي into ا to make past tense 'he came'
Changed vowels and added ت to show 'you' did the action
Added نا to show who was affected by the action (us)
Added أَ at the beginning to make it a question

steps.m: to turn or divert something from its path

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters into past tense verb
Changed to present tense form by adding ت prefix and changing internal vowels
Added نَا to show who is being turned/diverted (us)

steps.m: in order to turn us away (purpose of أَجِئْتَنَا - have you come to us)
steps.n: Added لِ at the start to mean 'in order to' - shows this is the purpose or goal of their coming
This word combines two parts: عَنْ meaning 'about/away from' and مَا meaning 'what'
When عَنْ comes before مَا, the ن changes to match the م sound and doubles it (shown by the ّ mark). This is a common Arabic rule to make words flow better when speaking.
Adding fatha vowels creates the basic past tense active verb form
Changed the last vowel to sukun (ْ) to prepare for adding نا
Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action
This is the basic preposition meaning 'on' or 'upon'. Since it's a particle, we start with its basic form rather than a root.
The ى changes to يْ to prepare for connecting with the next part. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.
Added هِ at the end which means 'him'. This shows who the action is happening to.

steps.m: to nurture/care for (as a father does)

steps.n: Root letters أ-ب-و become the simple word for 'father'
Changes from 'father' to irregular plural 'fathers' - not a simple plural like adding -un
Adds 'our' and shows this is what was found in the phrase 'what we found'
Added vowels to make basic past tense verb
Changed vowels for present tense subjunctive form with weak middle letter
Adds 'you' prefix to make it second person

steps.m: and that it be/exist (subjunctive of يَقُولُ)
steps.n: Added و conjunction at beginning to connect to previous phrase
Added fatha to show the vowel sound on the preposition ل

steps.m: for/to you two (addressing both Moses and Aaron)
steps.n: Added كُمَا which shows this is addressing exactly two people. In this ayah, it's referring to Moses and Aaron when the disbelievers are speaking to them.
Basic meaning of the root
The vowels show this word means 'became big/great' rather than just 'being big/great'
Changed the pattern to make an abstract noun showing the quality of being great
Added 'the' (ال) at the start to make it specific, with a longer 'aa' sound at the end
Added damma to show this is the subject doing something in the sentence
Basic preposition showing location or being inside something. Here it connects 'the greatness' to 'the earth', showing where they want the greatness to be.

steps.m: earth, land, ground (as a basic concept)
steps.n: Basic meaning of the root
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the word for 'earth'
Adding a small 'i' sound at the end to show the earth belongs to or is connected to something

steps.m: the earth (in فِى 'in' from previous word)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it specific - 'the earth'. This word follows فِى (in) from the previous word, showing location.
Added fatha to show clear pronunciation

steps.m: and what (connected to أَجِئْتَنَا - have you come to us)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this question with the previous phrase. In this ayah, it's connecting to their first question 'have you come to us?'
Basic form of the first person plural pronoun meaning 'we'
Added the ُ (damma) at the end to show this is the subject form of 'we'
Added fatha to show the basic preposition meaning

steps.m: for/to you both (referring to Moses and Aaron)
steps.n: Added كُمَا which shows this is addressing exactly two people. In this ayah, it's referring to Moses and Aaron when the disbelievers are speaking to them.
This is the basic past tense form of the verb
Adding vowel marks to show it's a past tense feeling verb
Changes from a verb to a noun meaning 'one who does the action'. The ؤ appears because of rules about hamza placement.
Makes the word plural to show many believers
Adds بِ at the beginning to show 'with' or 'in'
Added short vowels (fatha) to make it past tense 'he said'
Changed from قَوَلَ to قَالَ by replacing weak letter و with ا
Added وَ at the beginning to mean 'and'. This word connects to فِرْعَوْنُ (Pharaoh) who is the one doing the saying.
This is a borrowed word from ancient Egyptian language that became part of Arabic. While it follows Arabic word patterns, it doesn't have an Arabic root.
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the word correctly. The ِ (kasra) under ف shows 'i' sound, and ْ (sukun) on ر shows no vowel.
Added ُ (damma) at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in the sentence - Pharaoh is the one who said something.
Changed to basic command form. The hamza changes to match the vowel sound.
Adds 'و' at the end to make it a command for many people instead of one person

steps.m: bring to me! (command to many people)
steps.n: Added نِى (nee) at the end means 'to me' - changes 'come!' to 'bring to me!'
The doubled middle letter makes the meaning stronger and more complete
The kasra at the end shows this word is connected to سٰحِرٍ (magician) that comes after it
The بِ at the beginning means 'with' - Pharaoh is asking to be brought 'with every' knowledgeable magician
Added short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he did magic'
Changed pattern to show someone who does the action as their role
Added tanwin kasra to show this is indefinite (a magician) and comes after the word كُلِّ (every/all) in the sentence
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Adding ي between second and third root letters changes this from a verb to an intensive adjective
Adding kasra tanween shows this word describes سٰحِرٍ (magician) in the phrase 'every knowledgeable magician'
Describes someone who is very knowledgeable - here it's owned/possessed by something (shown by the -in ending)
Added مَّا to the basic particle ل to create a word meaning 'when'

steps.m: Then when (connects to جَآءَ which comes next)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens right after something else. This word connects to جَآءَ (came) which comes right after it.
Added vowel marks (fatha) to the root letters to make it mean 'he came' in the past tense
Changed to hollow verb pattern by adding alif after first letter
Extended the alif to show emphasis/intensity of coming
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Adding ة to the end changes it from 'magic' to mean 'those who do magic'
Added ال to point to specific magicians and doubled the س with shadda
Added ُ (damma) at the end to show these magicians are the main doers of the action
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
The و changes to ا because it's a weak letter
Basic preposition showing direction or purpose

steps.m: to them (referring to السَّحَرَةُ - the magicians)
steps.n: Added the pronoun هُم (them) to show who the preposition is directed to. This refers back to the magicians (السَّحَرَةُ) mentioned earlier in the ayah.
This is the basic form of the proper name borrowed from Hebrew, meaning Moses
Added basic vowel marks to show pronunciation: damma (ُ) on م and fatha (َ) on س
Added shadda (ّ) on م to show emphasis in pronunciation
Added alif khanjariya (ىٰ) to show proper pronunciation of the final sound
Added elongation mark (ٓ) to show this is addressing Moses directly in speech
Added أَ at beginning to make it a proper command form

steps.m: throw! (all of you) (command to the magicians)
steps.n: Added وا to make it plural - now it's addressing multiple people. This is Musa speaking to the magicians.
Base interrogative word meaning 'what'
Added fatha to show correct pronunciation
Extended the alif to show it's a question word. In this ayah, Musa is asking the magicians about what they are going to throw.
This is the basic form of the pronoun used when talking to someone
Adding م at the end makes it refer to multiple people. In this ayah, Musa is speaking to all the magicians.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The ي changes to ى because it's at the end.
Added مُ at start to make it mean 'one who does the action', changed vowels to show it's a doer
Changed to plural form by adding ون and changing the kasra to damma

steps.m: those who are throwing (in response to موسى's command)
steps.n: Added shadda on م and fatha on ن. This word shows the magicians responding to Moses's command to throw their ropes and staffs.
This is the base word meaning 'when' that shows something happened after another thing
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows something that happened before
Changed the final ا to آ, which is just a writing variation that doesn't change the meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adding 'أ' at the start makes the throwing action more forceful and deliberate. Like the difference between tossing something vs really throwing it.

steps.m: they threw (referring to the magicians in the story of Moses)
steps.n: Adding وا shows multiple people did the action. This refers to when the magicians threw down their ropes and staffs in their contest with Moses.
Added vowel marks to show past tense and who did the action
Changed middle weak letter و to ا, which is common for hollow verbs

steps.m: Moses (the one speaking to the magicians)
steps.n: This is a proper noun borrowed from Hebrew, referring to the prophet Moses. In this ayah, he is speaking to Pharaoh's magicians after they performed their magic tricks.
This is a question word used to ask about things. In this ayah, Musa is asking about what they have brought, which is later identified as magic (السِّحْرُ).
This is the basic meaning of the root
The root letters ج-ي-أ become جِئْ (came) by adding a small line under ج and changing ي to ئ with two dots above
Added تُم at the end to show that multiple people are being addressed. In this ayah, Musa is speaking to the magicians who had just thrown down their ropes and staffs.
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning 'with' or 'by'
Added the pronoun هِ (it) to show what is being referred to. In this ayah, it refers back to السِّحْرُ (the magic) that was mentioned right after.
This is the basic meaning of the root
Root letters get vowels to make the noun meaning 'magic'
Adding definite article makes the noun definite

steps.m: the magic/sorcery (subject of what Musa is talking about)
steps.n: The ُ shows this is the subject being discussed in Musa's statement
This is the basic form of the emphasis particle that introduces important statements

steps.m: indeed/certainly (emphasizing what Allah سَيُبْطِلُهُ will nullify)
steps.n: Adding the shadda makes the meaning stronger and shows that what follows is a definite statement, not a condition. In this ayah, it emphasizes that Allah will certainly nullify the magic.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity'
The small alif makes the 'aa' sound longer
Makes the word definite by adding 'the'
Shows this word is affected by something before it in the sentence
This is the basic meaning of the root
Root letters got vowels added to make a past tense verb meaning 'it became invalid'
Added أ at beginning to show making something happen
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Added سَ to show it will happen in the future

steps.m: he will make it invalid (referring to السِّحْرُ/the magic)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show what is being made invalid (referring back to the magic mentioned earlier in the verse)
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it. Here it makes the statement about Allah stronger and more certain.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity'
Changed from the general word for 'god' to become Allah's unique name by adding ال, where the letters merged together in a special way
Added fatha at the end because it comes after إِنَّ (indeed/verily). The hamza at the start is written as ٱ.
This is a negative particle that makes the verb يُصْلِحُ (improves/fixes) negative, changing its meaning to 'does not improve/fix'
Added fatha mark to show correct pronunciation
The vowel pattern fa-lu-ha (َ_ُ_َ) shows something becoming good on its own, rather than being fixed by someone else
Added hamza at beginning to show making something else good, not becoming good oneself
Present tense verb showing ongoing action. Takes عَمَلَ (deeds) as its object - meaning he makes the deeds good/proper.
Adding vowels creates the basic past tense verb
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added مُ at the start and changed vowels to show who does the action
Added ين to make it plural, referring to multiple corrupters
Added ال to make it specific and َ to show it's the object of the verb يُصْلِحُ (he improves/fixes)
Added vowels (a) and doubled the last letter to make 'he made true'
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels. This shows Allah is doing the action now.

steps.m: and he makes true (subject of Allah)
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect this word to what came before it
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the word for 'deity'
Added the U mark at the end to show this is the subject
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
The middle letter is doubled to add emphasis and make it a noun meaning 'truth'
Added ال to point to a specific truth (related to يُحِقُّ 'establishes' earlier in the ayah)

steps.m: the truth (object of يُحِقُّ - what Allah establishes)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is what's being established by Allah in the phrase يُحِقُّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْحَقَّ (Allah establishes the truth)

steps.m: to speak or communicate with words

steps.n: Changes from the action of speaking to a single spoken thing (one word). The ة shows this is one specific instance.
Changes from singular (one word) to plural (many words) by adding ات at the end
Added -i ending because it's connected to another word that owns it
Added بِ at the beginning to show these words are being used as a tool or means ('by/with'). This connects to the verb يُحِقُّ (establishes) to show how Allah establishes the truth.
Added هِۦ at the end to show these are His (Allah's) words. The ۦ is just a pronunciation helper.
This is a conditional particle that introduces a hypothetical situation. In this ayah, it introduces the idea that even if the wrongdoers dislike it, Allah will still establish the truth.

steps.m: and even if (connects to وَيُحِقُّ - and He establishes)
steps.n: Added 'and' to connect this phrase to the previous statement about Allah establishing the truth. The full meaning is that Allah will establish the truth even if the wrongdoers dislike it.
Added vowels to show this is past tense and the subject is 'he'. This word comes after وَلَوْ (even if/although) in the ayah.
Adding مُ at the start and ِ in the middle makes this describe the person who does the action. Like how 'crime' becomes 'criminal' in English.
The ونَ ending shows we're talking about many people (all male or mixed group), like adding 's' in English to make things plural
Adding ٱلْ (al-) at the start is like adding 'the' in English - it shows we're talking about specific criminals mentioned in this context
Added fatha to show the correct pronunciation
Added the elongation mark (آ) to show the long vowel sound
Added فَ at the beginning to connect this idea with what came before. This shows sequence or result, like saying 'and then' or 'so then' in English.
These separated letters represent the basic meaning of safety and trust
Basic root letters got short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make past tense verb meaning 'he was safe'
Changed the first letter to hamza and added alif, which makes the meaning about actively choosing to trust/believe rather than just being safe
This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew/Egyptian, so we start with the base form
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation
Added لِ at the beginning to show this is the indirect object of ءَامَنَ (believed). The ٰٓ at the end shows the word is being paused on in reading.
This is a complete particle that shows an exception. In this ayah, it connects to the word ءَامَنَ (believed) to show that only some people believed, not everyone.

steps.m: to spread out or scatter (like seeds or small things)

steps.n: Root means 'to scatter' - doubled middle letter and added damma to make it mean 'tiny scattered things' (like dust or seeds)
Adding يّ changes it from 'scattered ones' to mean 'descendants' (like scattered seeds that grow)
Adding ة at the end makes it mean 'one line/group of descendants' instead of just 'descendants' in general

steps.m: offspring (as a complete group, subject of آمَنَ)
steps.n: Added tanween to show this is the complete group that believed in Moses. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb آمَنَ (believed) in this ayah.
Basic preposition showing where something comes from or showing part of something
Added shadda to show strong connection with next word. This preposition connects to فِرْعَوْنَ (Pharaoh) to show who they were afraid of.

steps.m: to stand/rise up (especially as a group)

steps.n: Changes the concept of 'standing together' into a noun meaning 'people who stand together' - a nation or community
Added هِ ('his') to show these are Musa's people being referred to
Added ۦ after هِ to show proper elongation of the possessive 'his'
Basic preposition showing something is on top of or over something else
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end to show this is the standard written form. In this ayah, it connects to the word خَوْفٍ (fear) to show what they were upon/over.
Root letters turned into a noun meaning 'fear' by adding the vowel marks. The middle letter و stays silent.
Added tanwin kasratayn (two kasras) at the end to show this is indefinite (like 'a' or 'an' in English) and is being acted upon by the word 'from' (مِّن) before it in the ayah.
This is a basic particle that shows where something starts or comes from. Here it shows the offspring came from his people.
The dot under the م gets a shadda mark (ّ) to help connect it smoothly with the word before it. The meaning stays the same.

steps.m: Pharaoh (the title of ancient Egyptian kings)
steps.n: This is a proper noun borrowed from ancient Egyptian. It was the title given to the kings of ancient Egypt.

steps.m: Pharaoh (object of fear in مِن فِرْعَوْنَ)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this is the object of the preposition مِن (from). In this ayah, it shows that the people were afraid of Pharaoh.
Adding the vowel marks 'fatha' (َ) turns the root letters into a past tense verb meaning 'he gathered'
The kasra at the end shows this is 'the group/assembly OF' someone or something
Added pronoun showing who the nobles belong to

steps.m: and their assembly/nobles (referring to Pharaoh and his chiefs)
steps.n: Added و to connect this word to what came before it in the verse

steps.m: that (introduces the fear of فِرْعَوْنَ testing them)
steps.n: This is a particle that connects two ideas by introducing what someone fears, thinks, or expects might happen. Here it connects the 'fear' (خَوْفٍ) to what they fear might happen - that Pharaoh would test/persecute them.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form past tense verb

steps.m: he tests/puts through trials (referring to فِرْعَوْنَ)
steps.n: Changed from past to present tense by adding يَ at the start. Shows Pharaoh is the one doing the testing/persecution.

steps.m: he tests/persecutes them (Pharaoh persecuting the believers)
steps.n: Added هُمْ to show who is being tested/persecuted - refers to the believers mentioned in the ayah
Starting with the base emphatic particle إنّ which adds certainty to what follows

steps.m: and indeed/certainly (connects to previous statement about Pharaoh)
steps.n: Adding و at the beginning connects this statement to the previous one about Pharaoh, like saying 'and' in English
This is the basic form of the proper noun for the ruler of ancient Egypt. The kasra and sukun help with pronunciation.
The fatha at the end shows this word is being acted upon by the word مِن (from) in this sentence

steps.m: to rise up or be high

steps.n: Added vowels and changed و to ٍ to show this describes someone/something that is high or exalted. This word tells us about Pharaoh - that he was high/exalted in the land.
Added لَ at the beginning to add emphasis, showing Pharaoh was definitely/truly high/exalted. This emphasis connects to إِنَّ earlier in the verse.
This is a basic preposition that shows location or containment. It's one of the most common Arabic words.
Added ى at the end to complete the preposition. In this ayah, it connects with 'the earth' to show where Pharaoh was acting arrogantly.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters together
Adding ال makes it specific - 'the earth' rather than just any earth
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word فِى (in) in the ayah
Starting with the emphatic particle إنّ which adds certainty to what follows
Adding هُ at the end to show we're talking about 'he' (referring back to Pharaoh)
Adding و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Added fatha at the end to help connect it to the next word smoothly
Added لَ at the beginning to add emphasis, showing that Pharaoh was definitely among the transgressors (ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start and changed vowels to make it mean 'someone who does this action'
Added ين to make it refer to many people who do this action

steps.m: the ones who go beyond limits (those who are mentioned in this ayah as being excessive in their opposition to Musa)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to point to specific people and added َ at the end to show this word completes the phrase لَمِنَ (is from)
Added basic past tense pattern with fatha marks
Changed to common past tense form with alif
Added و at beginning to connect this to what came before
This is the basic form of the proper name borrowed from Hebrew (מֹשֶׁה/Moshe). It doesn't follow Arabic root patterns since it's a borrowed name.
Added the basic vowel marks to show how the name is pronounced.
Added alif maqsura (ى) at the end, which is common for Arabic names and words ending in a long 'aa' sound.
Added a small alif (ٰ) above the alif maqsura to show it's pronounced as a long 'aa' sound. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb قَالَ (he said) in this ayah.
Changes verb root into a noun meaning 'a group that stands together'
Adds kasra at end to show this word is being possessed by something

steps.m: O my people! (Musa calling out to his people)
steps.n: Added يٰ at the beginning to show Musa is directly addressing his people. This is part of what Musa says in the ayah.

This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition - if something happens, then something else follows. Here it connects with تَوَكَّلُوٓا۟ (rely/depend) to say 'if you have believed, then rely...'
Changed root letters into a command by adding damma (u) and sukun (stop mark) - like saying 'BE!'
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' (not 'you') did this in the past (I was)

steps.m: you all were (addressing the people of Moses)
steps.n: Changed from 'I was' to 'you all were' by changing تُ (meaning 'I') to تم (meaning 'you all')
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make basic past tense verb

steps.m: he caused to be secure/he believed
steps.n: Adding long alif at start changes meaning to 'make someone/something do the action'
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did the believing (instead of 'he'), and the ا got longer to match

steps.m: you all believed strongly (addressing multiple people)
steps.n: Changed the ending from تُ (I) to تُم (you all) to show many people instead of one person
This is the base word - Allah's name. The shadda shows emphasis on the ل.
Adding the kasra (ِ) at the end shows possession or connection, like saying 'of Allah'
Adding بِ at the beginning shows how something is done or what it's done with. Here it shows trust being placed 'in' Allah.
Basic preposition meaning 'upon' or 'on'
Changed ى to ي with sukun to prepare for adding the pronoun
Added هِ at the end to show this means 'upon him'
Added فَ at the start to show this connects to the next word تَوَكَّلُوٓا۟ (put your trust), meaning 'so upon Him put your trust'

steps.m: to trust or depend on someone/something

steps.n: Adding the double mark (ّ) on ك shows doing something strongly or completely
Adding تَ at the start shows doing the action for yourself - so here it means putting your own trust in something

steps.m: put your complete trust! (command to a group)
steps.n: Adding وا at the end makes this a command to many people, like saying "all of you do this!"

steps.m: if (introducing the condition 'if you have believed')
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces the first part of an if-then statement. Here it connects 'if you have believed' with 'then upon Him rely'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he was' to 'be!' - a command telling someone to exist/happen right now
Added تُ at the end to change from 'you' to 'I' - like adding 'I' to show who did it

steps.m: you all were (past tense, referring to multiple people being addressed in إِن كُنتُمْ ءَامَنتُم - if you all have believed)
steps.n: Added م to make the verb plural, showing it's addressing multiple people. This word is part of a conditional phrase 'if you all have believed'

steps.m: to submit peacefully, surrender in peace

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Changed to active participle form showing who does the action
Added emphasis through shadda

steps.m: those who truly submit (as the object of كُنتُم)
steps.n: Added plural ending and case marker to show this is the object of كُنتُم (you were)
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb (has و in middle). This is a common pattern in Arabic.
Adding وا at the end makes it refer to a group of three or more people (they)

steps.m: so they said (following what happened before)
steps.n: Adding فَ at the start shows this happened as a result or sequence after something else
This is a basic preposition that shows something is on top of or dependent on something else. It's not derived from a root.
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation. In this ayah, it connects to the word اللَّهِ to show complete reliance upon Allah.
Added emphasis with shadda to show this is the ultimate, supreme deity
Added kasra at the end to show possession or connection, and changed alif to wasl-alif since it comes after another word

steps.m: to trust or depend on someone

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning stronger
Adding تَ at the start shows doing the action to yourself
Changed from past tense 'he trusted' to a command 'trust!' by removing the last vowel and adding a stop mark
Adding نا at the end shows 'we' did the action. This word connects to اللَّهِ (Allah) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create basic past tense verb
The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who nurtures and cares completely
Added نا at the end to show it belongs to us (our Lord). The word is being used to call out to Allah directly, like saying 'O our Lord!'
This is the basic negative particle that negates the verb تَجْعَلْنَا (make us) that comes after it
Added the fatha mark (َ) to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'laa'

steps.m: to make or set something as something else

steps.n: Added vowel marks (fatha) between the root letters to make it mean 'he made' in the past tense

steps.m: you make (in a command or conditional context)
steps.n: Changes from 'he made' to 'you make' by adding تَ at start. Used when giving commands or in if-statements
Verb 'make' with 'us' attached at the end to mean 'make us'
Changed from a root meaning 'test' into a noun meaning 'testing' by adding the vowel 'i' and making it silent with sukoon
Added ة to show this is one specific instance of testing
Added tanween to show this is the object of the verb تَجْعَلْنَا (make us)
Connected letters with vowel marks. The root meaning of standing became associated with a group that stands together.
Added لِلْ (for the) at the beginning to show this is for or to the people
The ل at the start gets a shadda (doubled), and the end gets a kasra (i-sound) because it's connected to the word فِتْنَةً (trial) in the sentence
Added vowels to connect the root letters and show this happened in the past
Forms active participle pattern فاعل showing the doer of action. This word is in the genitive case because it is possessed by the word قوم (people), forming the phrase 'people of the wrongdoers'.
Added ين to show multiple wrongdoers. This word follows قَوْمِ (people) to show these are the wrongdoing people.
Added ال to make it specific and shadda because ظ is a sun letter
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he saved' to 'save!' with extra emphasis (like saying 'please save!')
Adding نا at the end means 'us' - making it 'save us!'
Added وَ to connect this command with what comes after it (بِرَحْمَتِكَ - by Your mercy)
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Adding ة transforms the root into the abstract noun meaning 'mercy'
The ك at the end means 'your', making it 'your mercy' instead of just 'mercy'

steps.m: by your mercy (connected to نَجِّنَا - save us)
steps.n: Added بِ at the beginning to show this mercy is the means by which the saving happens. This connects to the word نَجِّنَا (save us) in the ayah.
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows something coming from or out of somewhere else
Added fatha at the end to connect it smoothly to the next word الْقَوْمِ (the people). This combination means 'from the people'
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'people' by adding fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter. The meaning comes from the idea of people standing together as a group.
Added ال to specify these particular people rather than just any people
Added kasra at the end because this word comes after مِنَ (from) in the ayah. This shows the connection between the words.

steps.m: to reject, deny, or cover up truth

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and make a past tense verb
Changes verb into a noun meaning 'one who does the action'
Changes from one rejecter to many rejecters (makes it plural)
Makes the word definite and adds final vowel. The alif is written as ٰ (dagger alif) in this form.

steps.m: to reveal or inspire something to someone

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added أ at beginning to show causing something to happen. The و changed to a sukun because of this pattern.
Added نا at the end to show 'we' did the action. The last letter gets a sukun before نا.
Added و at beginning to connect to previous statement. The alif at the end is lengthened to show end of phrase.
This is a preposition that shows direction or destination. Here it connects to Moses (مُوسَىٰ) to show the revelation was directed to him.
Added an alif khanjariyah (small alif) above the final letter to show the correct pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
This is a proper name of Hebrew origin (מֹשֶׁה/Moshe) that has been naturalized into Arabic. It doesn't follow standard Arabic morphological patterns.
Added diacritical marks to show correct pronunciation: damma (ُ) on م and fatha (َ) on س
Added alif khanjariyah (ٰ) which is a small alif written above the final ى to indicate the long 'aa' sound at the end
The root letters أ-خ-و form the basic word for 'brother' (أَخ) when you add the fatha mark on top
Adding kasra at the end prepares the word to show possession
Adding ي at the end makes it mean 'my brother'
Added ه to show the brother belongs to Musa (موسى)
Added و to connect this word to the previous mention of Musa
This is the basic form of the word without any vowel marks. It's a connecting word that joins two parts of a sentence.
Added fatha (َ) on the first letter to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'anna'

steps.m: to settle or provide a place to live
to prepare or establish a place to live
steps.n: Doubling of و shows the action is done thoroughly and with purpose

steps.m: to establish a place for oneself
steps.n: Adding تَ shows they are doing this action for themselves

steps.m: you two (Moses and Aaron) establish places (for your people in Egypt)
steps.n: The ا at the end shows this command is for both Moses and Aaron together, as mentioned in the ayah
Changes verb root into a noun meaning 'a group that stands together'
Added kasra at the end to show this word is owned by or connected to something
Added كُمَا to show the people belong to two people (in this case, Moses and Aaron)
Added لِ at the beginning to show this is for/to the people

steps.m: to establish boundaries or settle in a place
steps.n: This root is related to setting boundaries and settling, which is why it came to be used for Egypt - a settled, bounded territory
The root letters that mean 'settling somewhere' become the name Egypt by adding specific vowels

steps.m: in Egypt (location where بُيُوتًا/houses are to be taken)
steps.n: Added بِ at beginning to mean 'in' and َ at end because it's being acted upon. This word shows where the houses mentioned next (بُيُوتًا) should be taken.
Root letters ب-ي-ت arranged with vowels to make the noun 'house' - a common pattern for nouns in Arabic
Changes vowels to damma and adds و to show multiple houses

steps.m: houses (as the object being settled in)
steps.n: Adds ًا to show this word is receiving the action of settling/taking as dwellings (تَبَوَّءَا)
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Changes from 'he made' to 'make!' by adding alif at start and changing the ending - turns it into a command to one person
Adds وا to make the command apply to a group instead of one person
Adds و at beginning to connect this command with previous statements in the verse
Root letters combined with vowel marks to make the noun meaning 'house'
Changes from 'house' to 'houses' by changing the middle vowels from 'ay' to 'uyuu'
Adds كُمْ suffix to show possession by multiple people ('your' plural)
Changed the verb into a noun meaning the direction something faces
Added ة to specify this is about a particular direction - specifically the direction Muslims face in prayer
Added tanween to show this is the object being made/appointed in the phrase 'make your houses a prayer direction'
Added vowels to connect the root letters
Added أ at beginning to make causative form. The middle و changed to ا due to weak letter rules
Changed to present tense form with damma ending. The middle weak letter changes from ا to ي.
Changed from 'I establish' to 'you all establish!' by adding وا at the end - this makes it a command to a group
Added و at beginning to mean 'and'
Root letters connected with short vowels (fatha) to form basic noun meaning prayer
Added ة with extended alif to make it refer to one specific instance of prayer
Added ال and shadda to specify this particular type of prayer and emphasize its importance
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of the command 'establish' (وَأَقِيمُوا۟)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
The shadda doubles the middle letter to make the verb stronger and turns it into a command
Added kasra at end to help with pronunciation when connecting to next word (المؤمنين)

steps.m: and bring good news! (command to المؤمنين)
steps.n: Added و to connect this command with previous commands in the verse

steps.m: one who trusts/believes (someone who has faith)
steps.n: Added مُ at the start and vowel marks to show this is about a person who does the action. Like changing 'trust' to 'truster' in English.

steps.m: believers (the ones receiving good news from بَشِّرِ)
steps.n: Added ين at the end to show we're talking about many believers. This word receives the action of بَشِّرِ (give good news to) in this ayah.

steps.m: the believers (the ones receiving good news from بَشِّرِ)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ at the start to mean 'the' and َ at the end because this word receives the action.
The root letters ق-و-ل become قَالَ (he said) by changing و to ا in the past tense
Adding conjunction و with fatha to connect to previous statement

This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew/Egyptian meaning 'drawn out of water'. It doesn't have an Arabic root. The ىٰ ending with alif khanjariya (dagger alif) is common in foreign names adapted into Arabic.

steps.m: to care for and help grow

steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this person does a lot of caring and protecting

steps.m: our caring Master (being called out to by Musa)
steps.n: The word is already complete with all necessary vowels and the shadda
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it, like adding 'truly' or 'certainly' at the start of a sentence
Added كَ at the end to show this is addressing Allah (like saying 'You' respectfully). This is part of Musa's prayer to Allah.
Basic root meaning
Changed to Form IV verb (made causative) by adding long alif after first letter. The final ى is because it's a weak verb ending in ي.
The final ى changes back to يْ before adding suffixes
Added تَ at the end to show 'you' (masculine singular) did this action. In this ayah, Musa is telling Allah that He (Allah) gave these things to Pharaoh and his chiefs.
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the word correctly: 'fir-'awn'

steps.m: Pharaoh (as the object of ءَاتَيْتَ - You gave)
steps.n: The fatha (َ) at the end shows that Pharaoh is receiving the action in this sentence - he was given something by Allah

steps.m: to fill or gather (in a group)
steps.n: Basic root meaning
Adding the vowel marks (fat-ha) turns the root letters into a past tense verb meaning 'he filled/gathered'
Adding هُ (hu) at the end shows what was filled (like 'it' or 'him')
Adding و connects this word to what came before it, meaning 'and'
Added kasra under first letter and connected letters to form basic noun
Adding -ah ending to make it mean 'one piece of decoration' instead of just 'beauty'
Added -an ending to show this is the thing being given/adorned with
Root letters م-و-ل were joined together with a long ا sound to make the word for 'wealth'
Forms broken plural showing multiple types or large amounts of wealth
Added tanween alif to show this is the object being acted upon
Added conjunction 'and' to connect to previous word (زِينَةً)

steps.m: in (location in الْحَيَوٰةِ الدُّنْيَا - worldly life)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows something is inside or within something else. Here it shows that the luxuries and wealth are in/during worldly life.
Root meaning 'live' changed to the noun 'life' by adding و and ة
Added ال to make it specific ('the life') and ِ to show it's connected to the word الدُّنْيَا (worldly) that comes after it
Root meaning 'near' becomes a feminine adjective meaning 'lower/nearer' by adding vowels and changing و to ي
Added ال to make it specific. The ل combines with د to make دّ. In this ayah, it describes الحَيَوٰةِ (life).

steps.m: to care for and help grow
he cared for and helped grow
steps.n: Adding fatha marks turns it into a past tense verb meaning 'he did this action'

steps.m: Our caring master! (being called by Musa)
steps.n: The root رَبَبَ (meaning 'to care for') becomes رَبّ (master/lord) with شدة, then نا (our) is added at the end to mean 'Our Lord'. Used as a respectful way to call Allah.

steps.m: to make someone lose their way

steps.n: Added يُ at start to show who is doing the action and when. Added ِ (kasra) after ض to show it's affecting others.

steps.m: in order to make (someone) lose their way
steps.n: Added لِ at start to show why they're doing this action - it shows their purpose

steps.m: so that they (Pharaoh and his chiefs) make (others) lose their way (from Your path)
steps.n: Added وا at end to show many people (Pharaoh and his chiefs) are doing this action together. This relates to فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَأَهُۥ (Pharaoh and his chiefs) mentioned earlier in the ayah.
This is a preposition showing movement away from something. In this ayah, it's used with يُضِلُّوا (they lead astray) to show what they are leading away from.
Added ي between second and third root letters to make the noun form
Added كَ at the end to show possession (your), and ِ before it because it comes after the word عَن (from/away from)

steps.m: to care for and help grow
Lord (the one who cares for everything)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who does complete and perfect caring

steps.m: Our Lord (being called upon by Moses)
steps.n: Added نَا at the end to show we are talking about our Lord. This is used when calling out to Allah in prayer or supplication.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters
Changed to a command form. This is Moses asking Allah to wipe out the wealth of Pharaoh and his people.
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows something is positioned above or on top of something else
Added ى at the end to help connect it to the next word. This preposition is connected to أَمْوٰلِهِمْ (their wealth) in this ayah
Added alif khanjariyah (small alif) above the ى to show it's pronounced with a long 'aa' sound
Added small vertical line (ٓ) to show the sound is stretched when reading
Changed the root letters م-و-ل into the noun مَال by making the و into an ا (alif) and adding vowels
Changed from single to plural by adding أ at start and و in middle - this is a common Arabic plural pattern
Changed the 'a' sound at the end to show it belongs to someone

steps.m: their wealth (referring to Pharaoh and his people)
steps.n: Added هِمْ at the end to show it belongs to them
This root carries the basic meaning of strength or intensity
Added vowel marks (fatha) to the root letters to make it mean 'he made strong' in the past
Changed vowels to 'u' sounds to make it a command telling someone to do something
Added helper alif at start to make the command easier to say
Added و at the beginning to mean 'and', and changed the helper alif to match

This is a basic preposition that shows something is on top of, over, or affecting something else. In this ayah, it connects 'wipe out' (ٱطْمِسْ) with 'their wealth' (أَمْوٰلِهِمْ), meaning 'wipe out upon their wealth'
The alif at the end is written with a small mark above it (called an alif maqsurah). This is just a writing variation and doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha and sukun to make the singular noun 'heart'. A heart is called this in Arabic because it can turn emotions and thoughts over.
Changed vowels to damma and added و to make it plural, meaning multiple hearts
Added هِمْ at the end to show these hearts belong to them (in this case, referring to Pharaoh and his people)
This particle connects to the previous statement about Musa's prayer, showing what happens as a result

steps.m: so they will not (connected to يُؤْمِنُوا)
steps.n: Adds the negation particle لا to show that the following action (believing) will not happen. This connects to the verb يُؤْمِنُوا (they believe) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from past to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing middle letter to ؤ to show ongoing belief/trust
Added وا to show multiple people doing the action
This is the basic form of the particle that shows continuation until a certain point
Adding shadda emphasizes the meaning
Adding ىٰ (alif maqsurah) at the end shows this connects to what comes next. Here it connects to يَرَوُا۟ (they see) to mean 'until they see'
Basic meaning showing the action of seeing
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create past tense verb
