Added يَ at the start to make it present tense (like 'sees' instead of 'saw'). The middle letter أ drops out because it's a special type of word, and the ending changes to ى.

steps.m: they see (until they see العَذَابَ الأَلِيمَ)
steps.n: Added وا to show multiple people doing the action. The ى changes to و because of the plural ending. This word connects to 'until they see the painful punishment' in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Long ا after second root letter makes this a noun meaning the result of causing pain/suffering. This word is what is being seen (object) in the phrase 'until they see the painful punishment.'
Made the noun definite with ال prefix
Added a fatHa mark to show this word is being acted upon (object)
Added vowels to connect the root letters and create basic past tense verb
Adding ي between second and third root letters creates intensive adjective form showing something that causes intense pain
Adding ال makes the adjective definite
Adding fatha shows this word describes العَذَابَ (the punishment) which is the object of يَرَوُا۟ (they see)
Added fathas to make it past tense. This word is connected to أُجِيبَت (was answered) as it shows who spoke.
Changed from قَوَلَ to قَالَ by replacing the weak letter و with ا, which is common for hollow verbs
This is a helper word that makes what comes after it stronger or more certain. Here it emphasizes that the prayer was definitely answered.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
Changed from active (he answered) to passive (was answered) by adding أُ at start and changing middle vowels to make it 'done to' instead of 'doing'

steps.m: was answered (referring to دَّعْوَتُكُمَا - your prayer)
steps.n: Added ت to show this refers to دَّعْوَتُكُمَا (your prayer) which is feminine
Changes the verb 'to pray' into a single prayer or one act of praying
Changes the ending ة to ت when connecting to pronouns, and adds ُ to show 'I' am doing the praying

steps.m: prayer of you both (subject of أُجِيبَت)
steps.n: Adds كُمَا to show the prayer belongs to both Moses and Aaron, who are being addressed in this verse. This prayer is the subject of the verb أُجِيبَت (has been answered)

steps.m: prayer of you both (subject of أُجِيبَت, emphatic)
steps.n: Adds emphasis by doubling the first letter with shadda
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added است at the beginning to mean 'seeking to' do the action. The base word means 'to stand straight', so together it means 'seeking to be straight'
Added ي in the middle to show a lasting state of being straight/upright
Added فَ to show this follows from something before it

steps.m: so you two seek to be straight
steps.n: The ا at the end shows this command is for two people
This is a negative particle that makes things not happen or denies them

steps.m: and not (connected to فَٱسْتَقِيمَا - so be steadfast)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this negative command with the previous command to be steadfast
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to follow'
Added ت at start to show 'you' as the doer in present tense
Added shadda to show intensive following
Added انِ to show two people doing the action
The alif is made longer and stronger to add extra emphasis to 'you two'

steps.m: to make a path or way

steps.n: Adds vowel marks to show this is describing a path or way
Adding ي lengthens the middle vowel to show this is the standard word for 'path/way'

steps.m: the path (object of تَتَّبِعَآنِّ - you two follow)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end because this word is being acted upon by the verb 'follow'
Special relative pronoun formed from demonstrative base with definite prefix and plural suffix. The lam gets a shadda due to assimilation with the definite article.
Basic negative particle that makes something 'not' happen. Here it's telling two people not to follow a path.
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form the basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, sukun on first root letter, and damma at end

steps.m: they know (those who لَا before it)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. This word refers back to those mentioned with لَا (not) before it in the ayah.

steps.m: to pass through or cross over
steps.n: This root gives the basic idea of moving from one place to another
Changed root to past tense 'made to pass' and added نا at end to show 'we' did it

steps.m: and we made (them) pass through (بِبَنِىٓ إِسْرٰٓءِيلَ)
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect with previous statement. This word shows how Allah helped the Children of Israel cross through the sea.
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb
The original notes are incorrect. بَنِي means 'children/sons of' and is different from بَنَى (he built). The بِ prefix was added to mean 'with/by the children'
The ى at the end shows possession/relation - 'children of' rather than just 'children'

steps.m: Israel (the name given to Prophet Jacob and his descendants)
steps.n: This is a proper name that comes from Hebrew. It was originally given to Prophet Jacob (peace be upon him) and later used for his descendants. The word appears in the Quran when talking about the Children of Israel (بني إسرائيل)
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun
Added the definite article ال to specify a particular sea
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being crossed. The word جٰوَزْنَا means 'we crossed/passed over' and ٱلْبَحْرَ is what was crossed.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show this is past tense

steps.m: he made (someone) follow/he pursued (فرعون acts on)
steps.n: Adding hamza at the start makes the verb causative - now it means making someone follow rather than following directly. In this ayah, Pharaoh (فرعون) is pursuing the Children of Israel, so the verb shows who is being followed using the accusative case.

steps.m: and so he pursued (فرعون acts on)
steps.n: The فَ shows this happened right after the Children of Israel crossed the sea

steps.m: and so he pursued them (فرعون pursued بني إسرائيل)
steps.n: The هُمْ refers to the Children of Israel (بني إسرائيل) who are being pursued
This is a proper noun title, likely of Egyptian origin that was adopted into Arabic
Added damma (ُ) at the end to show this is the subject doing the action of following in this verse
Added vowels to make the basic singular noun form meaning 'army'
Changes from singular 'army' to plural 'armies' by adding 'u' in the middle
Adds possessive pronoun meaning 'his'. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb 'followed' along with Pharaoh.
The word 'and his armies' is connected to Pharaoh - they acted together in chasing the people

steps.m: to wrongfully exceed limits or boundaries

steps.n: Connected the root letters together and added vowel marks to make it into a noun showing the act itself

steps.m: in transgression (describing how Pharaoh pursued them)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this describes how Pharaoh was pursuing them - he did it in a transgressive way
Changed the action into a noun showing the aggressive act itself. This describes how Pharaoh and his army chased after the Children of Israel.
Added -an ending to show it describes how something was done (like 'angrily' or 'quickly')

steps.m: and in transgression (describing how Pharaoh followed)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this with the previous word بَغْيًا (in tyranny)
This is a basic particle that shows a limit or endpoint in time or space
The shadda emphasizes the meaning, showing a definite endpoint
The alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end helps connect this word to what follows it. Here it connects to إِذَآ (when) to mean 'until when'
The small alif (ٓ) is added to help with pronunciation when continuing to the next word
This is the basic form of the word that shows time. It's a particle, so it doesn't come from a root.
The alif is elongated (آ) to add emphasis and show this is happening at a specific moment. In this ayah, it shows the exact moment when Pharaoh was drowning.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Added أ at the beginning to make it mean 'to make something happen' (like making someone catch up), and made the first letter quiet (with sukun)
Added هُ to show who was overtaken. In this ayah, this word describes what the drowning did to Pharaoh.

steps.m: to drown or sink in water
steps.n: This root relates to being submerged in water
Adding the vowel marks makes it a complete past tense verb meaning 'he drowned'
Adding ال (the) turns the verb 'drowned' into 'the drowning' - making it a noun that refers to a specific event
Added vowel marks to show this is about something that already happened
Changed middle weak letter و to ا, which is common in hollow verbs
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Adding hamza and alif at the start makes the word stronger - changing it from 'trust' to 'believe'. Like upgrading from a regular feeling to a stronger belief.

steps.m: I believed (said by Pharaoh when drowning)
steps.n: Adding تُ at the end shows that 'I' did this action. In this ayah, this is Pharaoh speaking when he realizes he's drowning.
This is a common particle used to start statements that need emphasis. In this ayah, it introduces what Pharaoh realized when drowning.

steps.m: that he (referring to the fact Pharaoh realized about Allah)
steps.n: The هُۥ (hu) attached at the end means 'it/he' and refers back to what Pharaoh realized
Added fatha to show the basic pronunciation of this negative particle
Extended the alif with madda (آ) to show elongated pronunciation. This word negates إِلٰهَ (deity) in the phrase 'there is no deity'.

steps.m: to be devoted to, to worship
one who is worshipped (object of لَا)
steps.n: Changed first letter to إِ and added vowels to create noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'. This word is negated by لَا (no) in this ayah.
Added a small alif (ٰ) above the letter ل to show it means 'deity/god'. The fatha (َ) at the end shows this word is being negated by لَا (no).
This is the base form of the exception particle that means 'except'
Added shadda on ل and alif at the end to create the complete exception particle. In this ayah, it's used to negate the previous statement لَآ إِلٰهَ (there is no god), creating the meaning 'except'.
Added elongation mark (ٓ) to show proper pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: the one who/which (refers back to إِلٰهَ - deity/god)
steps.n: Added ٱلَّ at the beginning. The ل merges with ذ creating a stronger sound (shown by ّ). This makes it specifically refer back to something mentioned before - in this case, the deity/god that was just mentioned.
Added alif at beginning and fatha marks to form the past tense verb meaning 'to believe'

steps.m: she believed (subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Added تْ at the end to show a female subject did this action in the past. This word appears after قَالَ (he said) showing what Pharaoh said when drowning.
This is a preposition that shows how something is done or connects things
Added هِۦ to show who or what is being referred to. This connects to the verb ءَامَنَتْ (believed) to show who was believed in.

steps.m: to be a son/child or build/establish

steps.n: The و at the end makes it a proper plural form
Adding ا after و completes the plural ending
This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew (ישראל/Yisrael). It combines two parts meaning 'struggles with God'.
Added fatha at the end because this word is being described by the word بَنِى (children of) that comes before it in the ayah.
This is a personal pronoun that doesn't come from a root. In this ayah, Pharaoh is speaking these words as he's drowning.

steps.m: and I (connected to ءَامَنتُ - I believed)
steps.n: The و connects this to Pharaoh's previous statement 'I believed'. He's saying 'I believed... and I [am from the Muslims]'
This is a basic preposition that shows where something comes from or what group it belongs to
Added fatha at the end because it connects to the next word (المسلمين). This helps the words flow together smoothly when reading.
This pattern makes the action into a noun
Adds مُ at the start and ِ in the middle to show someone who does the action of submitting
Adding ين at the end makes it refer to many people. This ending also shows it follows the word مِنَ (from) in this ayah.
Adding ال at the start makes it specific (like 'the' in English). The َ at the end shows it follows the word مِنَ (from).

steps.m: to come at the right time
steps.n: This is the root that gives us words related to time and presence

steps.m: to come at the right time
steps.n: Adding the long alif at start makes it into a complete word
Adding ال makes it specific to the current moment
Adding the hamza with fatha at the start turns this into a question with a tone of disbelief or sarcasm, like Pharaoh is saying 'Oh, NOW you believe?!'
This is a particle that adds emphasis or indicates completion of an action
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. This و means 'and' and joins this statement to the previous question ءَآلْـٰٔنَ (Now?)

steps.m: to disobey or rebel against authority
steps.n: This root is about disobedience and going against authority
Changed from basic root to past tense 'he' form by adding fathas and changing the last letter to ى
The ى changes back to ي with sukun before adding a suffix
Added تَ at the end to show 'you' (masculine singular) did this action in the past
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and form the basic noun meaning 'before'

steps.m: before (as the subject in this phrase)
steps.n: Added damma at the end to show this word is the subject in the phrase 'before (this time)'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
The word changes from 'he was' to a command 'be!' by dropping the middle و and changing the vowels

steps.m: you were (referring to Pharaoh in ءَآلْـٰٔنَ وَقَدْ عَصَيْتَ قَبْلُ)
steps.n: The تَ shows this is talking about 'you' (masculine) in the past. In this ayah, Allah is addressing Pharaoh about his past actions.
The وَ connects this to the previous phrase, showing this happened along with something else mentioned before
This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or what group something belongs to. In this ayah, it connects to ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (the corrupters) to show Pharaoh was from among them.
The َ (fatha) at the end helps connect it smoothly to the next word ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (the corrupters)
This root is about making things bad or causing trouble
The مُ at the start and ِ in the middle work together to show who does the action (like adding -er in English)
The ين ending shows we're talking about many people (like adding -s in English)

steps.m: the ones who cause damage/harm (object of مِنَ)
steps.n: Adding ٱلْ at the start and َ at the end shows we're talking about specific harmful people. This word comes after مِنَ (from/of) in the ayah.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it a proper word
Adding 'ال' makes it specific - we're talking about a particular day

steps.m: so today (referring to the day of punishment)
steps.n: Adding فَ connects this to what happened before, showing this is the consequence. The word refers to the day when Pharaoh's punishment came.

steps.m: to rescue or deliver from danger

steps.n: Added a shadda (double letter) to show doing lots of rescuing/saving. The و at the end became ى
Added نُ at start to show 'we' are doing the action, and changed middle vowel to kasra
Added كَ at the end to show who is being saved (you). This is Allah speaking to Pharaoh about saving his body.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic noun
Added kasra to show this word follows a preposition
Added ك to show the body belongs to the person being addressed
Added the preposition ب which means 'with/by'. This word explains how Allah will save Pharaoh's body (نُنَجِّيكَ means 'We will save you')
Added basic vowels to make the command form
Added تَ at the start to show who is doing the action (you)

steps.m: so that you become (purpose for نُنَجِّيكَ - We will save you)
steps.n: Added لِ to show purpose - this explains why Allah is saving Pharaoh's body (the action in نُنَجِّيكَ)
This is a question word asking 'who'. The vowel marks help show it's a question word rather than other meanings of these letters.

steps.m: for who (connected to خَلْفَكَ - behind you)
steps.n: The preposition لِ is added to show this word connects to خَلْفَكَ (behind you), indicating who will be behind.
Adding the vowel mark 'a' (fatha) to make it mean 'behind' as a place or location
Adding sukun makes this a noun meaning the space or area behind something
Adding fatha at the end shows this word connects to what comes next

steps.m: behind you (as a sign for those who come after you)
steps.n: Adds كَ to show this refers to Pharaoh being preserved as a sign for those who come after him
The root letters combine with the middle letter becoming a long alif. This is a common pattern for hollow roots (roots with ي or و in the middle).
The ة shows this is one specific instance of a sign

steps.m: a sign/miracle (object of لِتَكُونَ - for you to be)
steps.n: The ً shows this word is being acted upon by the verb لِتَكُونَ (for you to be)
This is an emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger, like saying 'truly' or 'indeed' in English

steps.m: and indeed (connects to لَغٰفِلُونَ - they are heedless)
steps.n: The وَ connects this word to what came before it in the ayah, showing these ideas are linked together
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
The pattern فعيل is used for adjectives that describe a strong or permanent quality
The ًا ending makes this word describe how an action is done
This is a basic word that shows something comes from somewhere or belongs to something, like saying 'from' in English
The double letter makes the word stronger and clearer to hear
The fatha at the end connects this word to the next word الناس (the people), making the complete phrase mean 'from the people'

steps.m: to be social or friendly with others

steps.n: Changes from verb (to be social) to noun (people who are social)
Added 'the' at the beginning and a kasra at the end because it follows the word مِنَ (from/of) in this ayah
Basic preposition showing separation or topic. In this ayah, it shows people being away from or distant from Allah's signs.
Added vowel marks to show pronunciation: fatha (َ) on ع and sukun (ْ) on ن. This preposition connects to the word ءَايٰتِنَا (Our signs) in this ayah.

steps.m: to show, appear, or be clear

steps.n: Adding ة makes this a feminine noun meaning 'a sign'
Adding نا shows possession - 'our sign'
Changes from singular 'sign' to plural 'signs' by adding ات
Adds kasra (ِ) to show the word is in genitive case
Adds a long alif (ٰ) to extend the sound of the word

steps.m: to neglect or be unaware of something
one who is heedless/unaware (predicate of إِنَّ)
steps.n: Added alif after first letter and kasra after second letter to show who does the action. This describes the people being talked about in the verse.

steps.m: those who are heedless/unaware (predicate of إِنَّ)
steps.n: Added ون to make it plural, showing it's talking about many people
Added لَ at the beginning to add emphasis, like saying 'surely' or 'indeed'
Changed alif to alif with overbar (ٰ) - this is just a writing variation that doesn't change the meaning
Added vowel marks to the base word قد which expresses certainty or completion of an action
Added ل at the beginning to add emphasis, like saying 'surely' instead of just 'certainly'
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it

steps.m: to settle or establish in a place

steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter changes it from just 'settling' to 'causing to settle'
Changed the last vowel to a stop (sukun) to connect with نا (meaning 'we')
Added نا to show 'we' did this action

steps.m: to build or establish a family line

steps.n: Added vowel marks (fathas) to the root letters to make it mean 'he built' in the past tense
Changed from 'he built' (a verb) to 'children' (a noun) by changing the middle vowel - a common pattern for family words
Added ي and ٓ (madd) to make the word plural - this is a common way to make plurals in Arabic

steps.m: Israel (proper name meaning 'one who strives with God')
steps.n: This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew (ישראל/Yisrael). It combines two Hebrew words: ישרה (yisra = 'to strive/wrestle') and אל (el = 'God'). It was the name given to Prophet Jacob (يعقوب) after he wrestled with an angel.

steps.m: to firmly establish (someone) in a place
steps.n: Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger and shows doing the action to someone else

steps.m: a place that has been established (for بَنِىٓ إِسْرٰٓءِيلَ)
steps.n: Adding مُ at the start shows this is describing the place where the settling happens. This word describes the place that was established for the Children of Israel.
Changed from basic root letters to the noun 'truth' by adding the vowel kasra (ِ) and sukoon (ْ)
Added tanween (two dots under) to make it indefinite - meaning 'a truth' instead of 'the truth'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added sukun to prepare for next suffix
Added نَا to show 'we' did the action
Added وَ at beginning to mean 'and'
Changed alif to alif maqsurah for pronunciation

steps.m: and we provided them with sustenance
steps.n: Added هُم at end to show who received the action (them)
This is the basic preposition meaning 'from'
The 'n' sound is doubled (has shadda) because it comes right after another word that connects to it
Added fatha at the end to connect it to the next word الطَّيِّبٰتِ (the good things)
Root letters get vowels and middle letter doubles to make a word meaning 'having good quality'
Added feminine plural ending ات to show multiple good things. The alif is elongated (ٰ) in this case.
Added 'the' (ال) at the start, which doubles the ط sound because they blend together

steps.m: of the good things (object of مِنَ)
steps.n: The kasra marks this noun as being in the genitive case because it comes after the preposition مِنَ (from). Prepositions in Arabic always put the following word in the genitive case.
This is a question word used to ask about things or situations

steps.m: and then what (connects to اخْتَلَفُوا - they differed)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this question follows from a previous statement. Here it asks about what happened after Allah settled the Children of Israel in a good settlement.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Added ٱ at start and ت after first letter to show people doing something back-and-forth with each other (like arguing or disagreeing)
Added وا at the end to show many people did this action together
Added fatha and shadda to create the basic particle form. The shadda shows emphasis on the ت.

steps.m: until (connects to جَآءَهُمُ - came to them)
steps.n: Added alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end to complete the particle. This word connects to جَآءَهُمُ (came to them) to show the endpoint of an action.
The root letters changed to make 'he came' by adding vowels and turning ي into ا
Extended alif (آ) adds emphasis to show the action was definite or significant

steps.m: definitely came to them (subject of العِلْمُ)
steps.n: Added هُمُ to show who was affected by the coming. In this ayah, it's knowledge (العِلْمُ) that came to them.
Changed the verb 'to know' into the noun 'knowledge' by adding the vowel ِ (kasra) under the ع and ْ (sukun) on the ل. This is like changing 'know' to 'knowledge' in English.

Added ٱلْ (al) at the beginning to mean 'the' and ُ (damma) at the end to show this is the main subject doing something in the sentence. This refers specifically to the knowledge that came to the Children of Israel.
Basic emphatic particle that introduces a sentence to emphasize what follows
Added shadda and kasra to strengthen the emphasis. This particle is emphasizing the word رَبَّكَ (your Lord) that comes after it.
The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who nurtures completely and perfectly

steps.m: the complete nurturer (acted upon by إِنَّ)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this word is being acted upon by إِنَّ (indeed/verily)
Adding كَ at the end shows this refers to your Lord
Added ي at beginning to show who is doing the action (he) and when (present/future)
Added ى at the end because this is a special type of verb that ends with a weak letter

steps.m: to separate or be between things

steps.n: The root meaning of separation becomes a word meaning 'between' - the space that separates things. This word is being acted upon by the verb يَقْضِى (He judges) in this ayah.
Added هُمْ at the end to show this is happening between 'them' specifically
Connected the letters together and added vowel marks to make the basic noun 'day'

steps.m: on the day (when Allah will judge between them)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word tells us when something happens. It connects to the next word (القيامة) to mean 'on the Day of Resurrection'
Changed و to ي and added alif to show it's a noun for the action
Added ة to make it refer to a specific instance - the Day of Rising

steps.m: the resurrection (connected to يَوْمَ - Day of)
steps.n: Added ال to make it definite and ِ to show it's connected to the word يَوْمَ (day) before it
Combines فِي (in) with مَا (what) to ask about the content or subject
Adding fathas (َ) after each letter makes it mean something that happened in the past
The و changes to ا because it's a weak letter in the middle of the word
Adding وا at the end changes 'he' to 'they'
This is a preposition that shows something is inside or within something else
Added هِ at the end to show what is being referred to (it)

steps.m: to differ or come after something

Added ٱ at start and ت after first letter to show people doing something back-and-forth with each other (like disagreeing together)
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
This is the basic conditional particle that introduces a possible situation

steps.m: then if (connects this condition to what came before)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this condition with the previous statement. This shows that one thing follows another.
Added basic vowels to connect the letters together
Added تَ at the end to show 'you' did this action in the past. The و is dropped because it's a weak letter between two vowels.
Basic preposition showing location or state. In this ayah, it connects to the word شَكٍّ (doubt) to show being 'in doubt'.

steps.m: to doubt or be uncertain about something

steps.n: The doubled middle letter makes the meaning stronger

steps.m: any doubt (that you have about فِى what)
steps.n: Added tanween (two dots under) to make it mean 'a/any doubt' instead of just 'doubt' - works with فِى to mean 'in doubt'
Starting with the basic preposition مِن which means 'from'
Added ما which means 'what' to specify what is being referred from
The ن merges with م due to a pronunciation rule when these letters come together

steps.m: from what (that we revealed to you)
steps.n: Added emphasis through shadda on first م and lengthened final alif. This word connects to أَنزَلْنَآ (we revealed) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Adding أَ at the start shows someone is causing the action to happen
Adding نا suffix changes the subject to 'we' and completes the perfect verb conjugation
Added long alif at the end for emphasis and pause in recitation
This is a preposition showing direction or movement towards something
The ى changes to ي with sukun to prepare for the attached pronoun

steps.m: to you (object of أَنزَلْنَا - we sent down)
steps.n: Added the pronoun ك at the end to show who is being addressed. This word shows who received what was sent down (أَنزَلْنَا) in the ayah.

steps.m: to ask or inquire about something
steps.n: This is the basic concept of asking questions or seeking information
Changes the basic root into a command by adding marks - a stop mark (sukun) on first and last letters, and a short 'a' sound (fatha) in middle
Adds a short 'i' sound (kasra) at the end to make the command flow better when speaking
Adds فَ at beginning to show this action follows from what was mentioned before - in this case, if you are in doubt about what We revealed to you
Added ال with a shadda to make it specific. This word is a relative pronoun that connects two parts of a sentence.
Added ن at the end to make it plural. This word refers to the people who read (يَقْرَءُونَ) the Book.

steps.m: to read or recite something carefully

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changed spelling to show proper hamza placement - doesn't change meaning

steps.m: they read/recite (referring to those who read الْكِتٰبَ - the scripture)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. This refers to the people who read the scripture mentioned in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Changed the pattern to make a noun meaning 'the thing that is written'
Added ال with sukun to make it specific. The alif has a wasl mark showing it connects to previous words.

steps.m: the book (object being read by يَقْرَءُونَ)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being read. The alif is written as a long alif (ٰ) in this Quran text style.

steps.m: from (connects قَبْلِكَ to what comes before it)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or starts. In this ayah, it connects the word قَبْلِكَ (before you) to the previous part of the sentence.
Added vowel marks (fatha and sukun) to the root letters to make it mean 'before'
Added ك at the end to show this means 'before you'. This whole phrase comes after مِن (from) in the ayah.
This is a basic particle that shows something definitely happened or is definitely true

steps.m: certainly, indeed (emphasizing جَآءَكَ - 'has come to you')
steps.n: Added لَ at the beginning to make the meaning stronger. This combination is used to strongly emphasize that something definitely happened. Here it emphasizes that the truth has definitely come.
The middle letter ي changes to ا (alif) because it's a hollow verb. Added fatha vowels to show past tense.
Added آ (alif with madda) which is just a writing variation of اا - it doesn't change the meaning
Added كَ at the end to show who was come to (you)
The doubled letter (shadda) shows this is a strong, definite truth, not just any truth

steps.m: the truth (that has come to you)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it refer to the specific truth mentioned in the ayah - the revelation that has come to Muhammad ﷺ

steps.m: the truth (that has come to you from your Lord)

This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it's used with قَبْلِكَ (before you) to mean 'from before you', referring to the previous people who read the Book.

steps.m: to nurture and develop something to completion

steps.n: Doubling the ب adds emphasis and turns it into a noun meaning 'one who does this action intensively'
The kasra shows this lord belongs to or is connected to something
Adding كَ shows this is your Lord. This comes after the word مِن (from) in the ayah.
This is the basic negation word used to say 'no' or 'not' in Arabic

steps.m: so not/then do not (connected to تَكُونَنَّ)

Adding تَ makes it mean 'to become' or 'to happen'
The نَّ makes the command stronger and more emphatic
ت at start shows this is 'you' doing the action
This is a basic particle that shows where something starts from or what group it belongs to
Added a fatha (َ) at the end because it connects to the next word ٱلْمُمْتَرِينَ (the doubters). This makes it easier to pronounce.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start and ت between م and ر to make it mean 'someone who doubts' instead of just 'doubted'
Added ين at the end to make it plural
Added ال at beginning to make it definite. This word comes after مِنَ (from/of) in the ayah.

The و at the beginning connects this phrase to what came before it. The whole phrase means 'and do not be' when combined with the following verb تَكُونَنَّ
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Added ت at start to show 'you' and changed vowels to show present tense

steps.m: you definitely are/become (negative command: 'do not be')
steps.n: Added نَّ at the end to add emphasis, often used with negative commands (لا)
This is a preposition that shows something is part of a group or comes from somewhere. In this ayah, it connects to الَّذِينَ (those who) to show who the person should not be among.
The َ at the end helps connect it smoothly to the next word
Starting with the basic demonstrative word and changing it to the relative pronoun form
Adding ال makes it specific, like saying 'the one' instead of just 'one'
Adding the proper pronunciation marks and changing the first letter to match how it's said

steps.m: those who (referring to those who denied Allah's signs)

steps.m: they strongly denied/rejected (the signs of اللَّهِ)
steps.n: The suffix وا shows a group of people (they) did this action. In this ayah, they are denying Allah's signs (بِـَٔايٰتِ ٱللَّهِ)

steps.m: to show, make clear, be evident

steps.n: Root letters were changed to make a noun meaning 'a sign' by adding a long alif and ta marboota at the end
Made the word plural to show multiple signs
Added بِ to show these are the signs that were denied, connecting to the verb كَذَّبُوا (they denied/rejected)
Added a kasra (i) sound at the end to connect it to 'Allah' - like saying 'signs of' instead of just 'signs'
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the basic noun form meaning 'a deity'

This is the unique name for the one true God in Islam. The ال makes it definite (like 'the'), the shadda emphasizes the ل, and the kasra at the end shows it belongs to or connects with something else in the sentence.
Added basic vowels to connect the letters and make it pronounceable
Adding تَ at the start changes it from 'being' to 'you will be' - like adding 'you' to the word

steps.m: and then you will be (among الْخٰسِرِينَ)
steps.n: The فَ shows this is a result or consequence of what came before. This word connects to الْخٰسِرِينَ (the losers) in the ayah.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or what group it belongs to
Added fatha at the end to connect it to the next word الَّذِينَ (those who). This shows we're talking about being 'from among those who'
The basic meaning is about losing something or experiencing a loss
The word changed from its basic root (lose) to 'he lost' by adding the vowels, showing it happened in the past
Changes from describing the action to describing the person who does it
Makes the word plural to show many losers
Adds 'the' to make it specific
Changes the alif to a long alif and adds a final mark to show this word is being acted upon by the verb تَكُونَنَّ (you would be)

This is the basic form of the relative pronoun used to refer to someone or something
Added ال (the) at the start and doubled the ذ to make it mean 'the one who' instead of just 'who'

steps.m: those who (plural, referring to the disbelievers mentioned in حَقَّتْ عَلَيْهِمْ)
steps.n: Added ين with a fatha at the end to make it plural, referring to multiple people. In this ayah, it refers to the disbelievers mentioned right after this word.
The middle letter is doubled (has shadda) to show intensity. This word describes كَلِمَتُ (word/decree) in this ayah.
The feminine marker ت shows this refers to كَلِمَتُ (word/decree) which is feminine
This is a preposition showing something is on top of or affecting something else
The ى changes to ي before adding pronouns at the end
Added هِمْ at the end to show this affects 'them'. This word connects to لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (they do not believe) by showing what is upon them.

steps.m: to speak or communicate with words

steps.n: Adding vowels makes this a past tense verb
Added ة to show this is one specific word or statement
The ة changes to ت before the ُ ending. The ُ shows this word is doing the action in the sentence - specifically, it's the subject of حَقَّتْ (became due/established)
Added basic vowels to connect the root letters
The root verb 'to care for' becomes a noun meaning 'one who cares for/nurtures' by doubling the middle letter. The doubled letter shows intensity and completeness of the nurturing.
Added kasra to show this word belongs to something after it
Added كَ at the end to show possession/ownership. In this case, it means 'of the word' rather than 'your', since it belongs to كَلِمَتُ (word) in this ayah.
This is the basic negative particle that makes the following verb يُؤْمِنُونَ (they believe) into 'they do not believe'
Added fatha mark to show the correct pronunciation
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb
Changed from past to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing middle vowels
Made it refer to multiple men by adding ون
This is a conditional particle that introduces hypothetical scenarios that didn't occur in the past

steps.m: and even if/even though (جَآءَتْهُمْ - came to them)
steps.n: The و (and) connects this concession to what came before it. This introduces a strong hypothetical - 'even if every sign came to them, they still wouldn't believe'
The root letters ج-ي-أ became جَاءَ (he came) by adding vowels and changing the middle letter ي to ا, which is a common change in Arabic verbs
The long alif (آ) makes the word stronger, like saying 'he really came!'
Adding ت with sukun (ـْ) changes it to feminine past tense

steps.m: she came to them (referring to those who reject in this ayah)
steps.n: Adding هُمْ shows who received the action - in this case referring to those who reject the signs mentioned in the ayah
The doubled middle letter makes the meaning stronger and more complete
Added damma (ُ) at the end to show this word is the subject of a sentence

steps.m: to show, make clear, or be evident

steps.n: Adding ة at the end turns this into a noun meaning 'sign'
Adding the two dots below (tanween kasra) shows this word is indefinite and in the genitive case

The words are actually the same - just written in two different ways. The first uses a special letter (آ) while the second breaks it into two parts (ءَا). Both show it's 'a sign' that belongs to something.
This is a particle that shows something continues until a certain point. In this ayah, it connects to the verb يَرَوُا۟ (they see), showing that something continues until they see something.
Added an alif marker (ٰ) over the ى to show it's a long vowel sound at the end. This doesn't change the meaning, it just helps with pronunciation.

steps.m: to see/perceive with eyes or mind

steps.n: Basic past tense pattern added vowels (fatha) to make 'he saw'
This is not a valid transformation. The verb 'to see' is رَأَى (ra'aa) and its present tense is يَرَى (yaraa). The given words are not correct Arabic forms.
Changed from singular 'he sees' to plural 'they see' by adding a waw (و) for the group and alif (ا) at the end
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make it a past tense verb

steps.m: punishment (the act or result of punishing)
steps.n: Long ا after second root letter makes this a noun meaning the result of punishment. This word is what is being seen in the phrase 'until they see the punishment'.
Adding ٱلْ makes it refer to a specific punishment being discussed
Adding َ shows this is what is being seen in the phrase 'until they see (يَرَوُا۟) the punishment'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
This form creates an intensive adjective that describes how painful something is. In this ayah, it's describing العَذَابَ (the punishment).
Adding the definite article makes it 'the painful'

steps.m: the intensely painful (describes العَذَابَ - the punishment)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end because this word is describing العَذَابَ (the punishment) which is the object of يَرَوُا۟ (they see)
This is a compound particle combining لَوْ (if) and لَا (not) to create the meaning 'why not'
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from what came before
Adding the second fatha (َ) completes the past tense verb pattern

steps.m: she was (referring to قَرْيَةٌ - a town)
steps.n: Added ت with sukun at the end to show the subject is feminine (referring to قَرْيَةٌ meaning 'a town' in this ayah)
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters
The ة changes the general concept into a specific place or instance
The tanween shows this is any settlement, not a specific one. This word is the subject of كَانَتْ (was/were) in this ayah.
Adding alif after first letter changes meaning from basic trust to religious belief/faith
Adding ت at the end makes it about a feminine subject (the town/قَرْيَةٌ in this ayah)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added ها to show who received the benefit

steps.m: and so it gave benefit to her (result of إِيمٰنُهَآ/their faith)
steps.n: Added فَ at the start to show this happened because of their faith (إِيمٰنُهَآ) mentioned in the ayah

steps.m: to trust, believe in, have faith

steps.n: Adding vowels to make it a complete past tense verb meaning 'he believed'
Changed from a past tense verb (he believed) to a noun meaning belief/faith, using a special pattern
Adds a 'u' sound at the end to show this word is doing the action
Adds possessive pronoun meaning 'her'. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb نَفَعَ (benefited).
This is the base form of the word that shows exception - it tells us something different happened in this case

steps.m: except (connects to قَوْمَ يُونُسَ - the people of Yunus)
steps.n: Added alif at the end to show this is connecting to what comes after it. In this ayah, it's showing that the people of Yunus were an exception to what was mentioned before.
Added vowel marks to create the noun meaning 'people' - those who stand/gather together
The ending َ (fatha) shows this word is receiving the action in the sentence. The word قَوْمَ is the object of the exception particle إِلَّا (except/but).
This is a proper name, referring to the prophet Jonah (peace be upon him). It comes from a non-Arabic origin.

steps.m: Yunus (Jonah) (object of إِلَّا قَوْمَ - except the people of)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end because this word is the object of the phrase 'except the people of'. In simple terms, something is happening to the people of Yunus in this sentence.
Starting with the basic negative particle لَمْ
These are completely different words with different meanings - لَمْ means 'not' for past actions, while لَمَّا means 'when/after'. They just look similar but aren't related.

steps.m: when, after, as soon as (connects to ءَامَنُوا۟)
steps.n: Adding the long alif at the end makes it specifically mean 'when/as soon as' something happened. Here it connects to ءَامَنُوا۟ (they believed), showing when they believed, something else happened.
This is the basic concept the word comes from
Adding the alif changes it from general trust to religious belief
Added وا at the end to show many people did this action together
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action. This word is connected to عَذَابَ (punishment) which comes after it in the ayah - it's what was removed.
This is the basic preposition meaning 'from' or 'away from'
Added هُمْ at the end to show who the preposition affects (them)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Added alif to make it a noun meaning 'punishment'. This word is the object of كَشَفْنَا (we removed), showing what was removed.
Changed from action to the resulting state or feeling. The ِ (kasra) and ْ (sukun) pattern makes it a noun.

steps.m: the disgrace (that was removed from قَوْمَ يُونُسَ)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ at start to mean 'the' and ِ at end because it follows عَذَابَ (punishment) in the ayah. This refers specifically to the disgrace that was lifted from the people of Yunus.

This is a preposition that connects two words, showing that something happens within a specific time or place. Here it connects 'punishment' (عَذَابَ) with 'life' (ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ) to show when the punishment happens.
This root represents the basic concept of living or being alive
The root letters get vowel marks (fat-ha) to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he lived'
Changed from 'he lived' (verb) to 'life' (noun) by adding و and ة
Added ال to make it definite and ِ to show it's connected to another word (الدنيا - worldly) in the ayah
Root د-ن-و meaning 'near' was made into دُنْيا (dunya) by adding special endings to make a feminine word meaning 'nearest/lower' - often used to mean 'this worldly life'
Added ال to make it specific. The د gets doubled (shadda) because it combines with the ل sound. In this ayah, it's describing الْحَيَوٰةِ (life)

steps.m: to let enjoy, give pleasure to

steps.n: Adding shadda (ّ) on the middle letter makes the meaning more intensive and emphasizes the giving of enjoyment
Adding نٰ shows that 'we' are doing the action of letting others enjoy
Adding هُمْ shows who receives the enjoyment - 'them'
Adds conjunction to connect with previous phrase
This is a basic preposition showing direction or movement towards something

steps.m: to, towards (connects to حِينٍ - a time)
steps.n: The small alif (ىٰ) at the end is just a writing style - it doesn't change the meaning. إِلَى still means 'to' or 'towards' either way. It connects with حِينٍ to mean 'for a time'
Root letters get vowels (kasra-sukoon) to make a noun meaning 'time' from verb 'to pass'
Adding tanween (two kasras) makes the word indefinite, like saying 'a time' instead of 'time'
Added vowel marks to show this is a conditional particle that introduces hypothetical situations (things that didn't actually happen)

steps.m: and even if (connecting to previous statement)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this condition with what came before. The combination وَلَوْ is stronger than just لَوْ alone - it means 'and even if' rather than just 'if'
Added short 'a' sounds (fathas) to each letter to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he wanted'
The weak letter ي changes to ا, which is a common change in Arabic verbs with ي in the middle

steps.m: one who completely nurtures and cares for others
steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shadda) shows this is someone who nurtures and cares for others in a complete way

steps.m: the one who nurtures (subject of شَآءَ - willed)
steps.n: The ُ shows this word is doing the action of willing/wishing (شَآءَ) in this ayah
Adding the 2nd person masculine singular possessive pronoun suffix كَ to indicate possession. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb شَآءَ (willed/wished).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create past tense verb
Added alif after first letter to make the meaning causative - changing from just feeling secure to actively believing

steps.m: surely would have believed (in response to لَوْ/if)
steps.n: Added لَ at beginning to show this is the result of a condition (responding to لَوْ/if earlier in the verse). This combination shows what would have happened if something else had occurred.
This is a relative pronoun used to refer to people. In this ayah, it refers to whoever would have believed if Allah had willed.
This is a basic preposition showing location or containment. Here it connects with 'the earth' (ٱلْأَرْضِ) to show where something is happening.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters together and make them pronounceable
Added 'al' to make it specific and 'i' at the end because it follows the word فِي (in/on/at). This word tells us where مَن (whoever) is located.
Added damma to show it's a noun meaning 'whole' rather than a verb
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject of the sentence
Added هُمْ to show who 'all' refers to. This word is the subject of the verb لَءَامَنَ (would have believed) in this ayah.

steps.m: to gather or bring things together

steps.n: Added ي in the middle and vowel marks to make a word meaning 'gathered' or 'all together'

steps.m: all together (describes how everyone in الْأَرْضِ would believe)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this word describes how the action happens. It's connected to لَءَامَنَ (would believe) and describes how everyone in الْأَرْضِ (the earth) would believe - they would do it all together.
Added vowel marks to the basic pronoun meaning 'you'. This word is the subject of تُكْرِهُ (you compel) in this ayah.
Added فَ (then/so) at the beginning to show this follows from the previous statement
Added أَ at the beginning to make it a question
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb showing a feeling

steps.m: he forced (made someone do what they dislike)
steps.n: Adding أ at the beginning changes 'to dislike' into 'to force' (make someone do something they dislike)

steps.m: you force (addressing Prophet Muhammad ﷺ about الناس)
steps.n: Changed to present tense with ت prefix for 'you' and vowel pattern changes. This word is asking about forcing people (الناس) in the ayah.

steps.m: to be social, friendly with others

steps.n: Changes from verb (being social) to noun (people/mankind)
Added ال to show we're talking about specific people. The ن gets a shadda because it comes after ال.
Added fatha to show the people are receiving the action of being compelled
Basic particle form showing a limit or endpoint
Adding the shadda strengthens the meaning to show a definite endpoint
Adding the alif maqsura (ىٰ) at the end makes it connect to what comes after it. Here it connects to يَكُونُوا۟ (they become/are)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
The يَ at the beginning changes it from past tense (was) to present/future tense (is/will be)
The وا at the end changes it from 'he becomes' to 'they become' - showing multiple people
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb showing a feeling of trust
Changed the word to show who does the action instead of the action itself
Added ين to show it means many believers. This word is being affected by the verb يَكُونُوا (they become) in this ayah.
This is a special word that makes statements negative. Here it's working with كَانَ to say something was not possible
The و at the beginning means 'and', connecting this phrase to what came before it

steps.m: to exist or be in a state

steps.n: Added vowel marks (fathas) to make it past tense - 'he/it existed'
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter between two fathas. This word comes before لِنَفْسٍ (for a soul) in the ayah.
Added fatha and sukun to make the root letters into a noun meaning one self or soul
Added لِ at beginning to mean 'for/to' and tanwin kasra at end to show this is any soul (not a specific one). This word is what the verb تُؤْمِنَ (to believe) applies to in this ayah.
This is a basic particle that connects two parts of a sentence, showing that the second part (to believe) depends on or follows from the first part (it was not)
Added a fatha mark (َ) above the أ to show how to pronounce it correctly

steps.m: to feel safe or trust in something

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

steps.m: he made someone feel safe/caused to trust
steps.n: Adding alif at start changes meaning to 'making someone do the action'
Adding ت at start changes 'he' to 'you/she', and vowel pattern shows present tense instead of past
This is a basic particle that shows exception. It has no root since it's a fundamental word in Arabic.
Adding shadda and alif makes the exception stronger and connects it to what follows. Here it connects to 'by permission' (بِإِذْنِ) to mean 'except by permission'.
Changed the action of giving permission into the noun 'permission' itself
Added بِ at the start to mean 'by' and ِ at the end because it's connected to the word اللَّهِ (Allah) that comes after it
Changed from 'a deity' to the specific name of The One True God by adding ال and merging letters

Added kasra at the end to show possession, changed alif to wasl-alif, and added shadda to emphasize the L sound. This word shows who the permission belongs to in the phrase بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ (by permission of Allah).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb

steps.m: he makes/puts (subject of يَجْعَلُ الرِّجْسَ - he puts the punishment)
steps.n: The يَ at the start shows it's happening now or in the future. This word is connected to الرِّجْسَ (the punishment) which comes after it in the ayah.

steps.m: and he makes/puts (subject of يَجْعَلُ الرِّجْسَ - and he puts the punishment)
steps.n: The وَ at the start means 'and'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
This is the noun form meaning the actual filth or impurity
Adding ال makes it specific and doubles the ر letter
The fatha shows this word is receiving the action of يَجْعَلُ (places/makes)

This is a preposition that shows something is being placed upon or affecting something else. In this ayah, it shows that something (ٱلرِّجْسَ - uncleanliness/punishment) is being placed upon those who don't use their minds.
This is the basic form of the word that means 'who' - it's a special word that connects descriptions to people or things
Adding ٱلّ at the beginning makes it point to a specific person or thing
Adding ن at the end makes it refer to many people. This word describes the people who 'do not understand' (لَا يَعْقِلُونَ) in this ayah.
This is a negative particle that makes the verb after it mean the opposite. In this ayah, it changes 'they reason' to 'they do not reason'
Added fatha mark to show how the word is pronounced
Connected the root letters together with fatha to make it a past tense verb
The يَ at the start shows it's happening now or in the future

steps.m: they use their minds (those who لَا do not)
steps.n: The ونَ shows this is about a group of people. In this ayah, it refers to those who do not use their minds to understand.
The middle letter و is dropped and we add damma and sukun to make it a command form. This tells one person to do something.

steps.m: say! (command to one person, followed by انظُرُوا)
steps.n: The kasra at the end connects this command to what follows it (انظُرُوا - 'look!'). This is a common pattern when two commands come together.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Changed from 'he looked' to 'look!' by adding U sound at start and making it a command to one person

steps.m: all of you look! (commanding multiple people)
steps.n: Changed to plural command by adding وا and changing the final vowel. The hamza at start changed to wasl form (ٱ) because it's a command.
This is the basic question word that asks about things

steps.m: what (asking about something in ٱلسَّمٰوٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ)
steps.n: Adding ذَا (meaning 'this') makes the question more specific, like asking 'what is this?' instead of just 'what?'. Here it's asking about what can be seen in the heavens and earth.
This is a basic preposition that shows location or position. In this ayah, it connects to the word ٱلسَّمٰوٰتِ (the heavens) to show what is being looked into.

steps.m: to rise up or be elevated

steps.n: Adds اء to make it a noun referring to something high up
Changed to plural form by adding ات and inserting و to maintain the long vowel sound
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the س (shown by ّ) because it comes after ال
Changed spelling to use long alif (ٰ) instead of َا and added kasra (ِ) at the end because it follows the word فِى
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it a noun
Adding ال makes it specific
Added وَ (and) at start and ِ at end because it follows فِى (in/on)
Added fatha to show this is the question word 'what'. This word is the subject of تُغْنِى (benefit/avail) in this ayah.
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb
Changed from past tense 'he' to present tense 'she' by adding ت at start
Adding ة at the end turns this into a noun meaning 'a sign'
Adding ُ shows this word is in the nominative case (subject)
Changed to plural form by replacing ة with ات and extending the alif
Added ُ to show this is the subject doing the action in تُغْنِى (avail/benefit)
Added ٱلْ to make it specific/definite. This word is the subject of تُغْنِى (avail/benefit)
Added damma vowels to make it a plural noun for warnings
Added ال to make it specific and shadda on ن because it comes after ال
Added و to connect this word with what came before it in the verse (the signs/آيات)
Added damma to show this word is a subject together with آيات in the phrase 'the signs and warnings do not benefit'

steps.m: from/away from (connects قَوْمٍ to what comes before)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows separation or moving away from something. In this ayah, it connects the word قَوْمٍ (people) to what comes before it, showing who the signs and warnings don't benefit.
The root meaning of standing together becomes a noun for a group that stands united. This word follows عَن (from/about) in the ayah.
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) to show this word is indefinite (any people) and is affected by the word عَن before it
This is the basic negative particle that makes statements negative
The shadda (ّ) adds emphasis to the negation, making it stronger. This word is negating the verb يُؤْمِنُونَ (they believe) that comes after it, making the meaning 'they do not believe at all'

steps.m: to cause to feel secure, to make believe
steps.n: Adding long alif at start changes meaning to 'make someone do the action'
Changed from past tense (he believed) to present tense (he believes) by adding يُ at start and changing middle vowels
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
This is a question word that can't be broken down further

steps.m: so do/does/is/are (connecting to يَنتَظِرُونَ - they await)
steps.n: Adding فَ at the start shows this question is a result or consequence of something mentioned before. Here it connects to the next word يَنتَظِرُونَ (they await).
Added vowels to connect the letters and make a past tense verb
Added ن and ت to make it mean 'waiting with expectation' - like when you're specifically waiting for something to happen
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added ون to show multiple people are doing the action
Added shadda (ّ) to emphasize the meaning. This makes the exception stronger.
The word 'except' (إلا) is spelled with an alif at the end. It works with 'they await' to tell us what is not being awaited.

steps.m: to be like or similar to something
steps.n: This root is about things being alike
Added vowels to make a noun that means 'likeness' or 'similarity'
Added fatha at the end to show this word is describing the 'days' that come after it in the ayah
This root is about time passing
Added short 'a' (fatha) and silent mark (sukun) to spell out 'day'
Adding أ at the start is the first step in making this type of plural
Adding ا in the middle completes this plural form for 'days'
Added kasra at end to show this word is connected to 'those' (ٱلَّذِينَ) that comes after it
Starting with the basic demonstrative ذا and changing it to the relative pronoun form ذِي
Adding ين to make it plural, showing it refers to multiple people
Adding ال and shadda to make it definite and emphasize its role as a relative pronoun. This word is the subject of خَلَوْا۟ (passed away) in this ayah.
The alif at the beginning is written with a waṣla mark (ٱ) because it connects with the previous word in pronunciation
This root is about something being empty or passing
Added fathas and changed final و to ا to make past tense 'he' form
Adding و changes the subject from 'he' to 'they (masculine plural)'
Adding ا completes the past tense plural masculine ending وا
The small circle (۟) shows this is a plural 'they' ending

steps.m: from (connecting قَبْلِهِمْ to what came before)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or connects parts of a sentence. Here it connects قَبْلِهِمْ (before them) to the previous part.

steps.m: to be in front of, to precede
steps.n: This root is about things happening before other things
Changes the root into a basic noun meaning 'before'
Added kasra to show this word is possessed by what comes after it
Added هِمْ which means 'their/them', showing who this word relates to
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

steps.m: say! (command to يَنتَظِرُونَ - those who wait)
steps.n: Changed to a command form by: 1) dropping the middle weak letter و, 2) adding damma on ق, and 3) adding sukun on ل. This is telling one person to speak, connecting to those mentioned as 'waiting' in the ayah.
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Adding 'in' at start and 't' in middle shows someone is waiting for something specific to happen to them
Changed from past tense 'he waited' to a command 'wait!' by changing the first vowel to u- and making the middle vowel -i-
Changes from commanding one person to wait, to commanding many people to wait (adds فَ meaning 'so/then')
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it, like adding 'indeed' or 'truly' in English

steps.m: indeed I (speaking to مَعَكُم - with you)
steps.n: Adding ى at the end shows this is about 'me/I'. This word connects to مَعَكُم (with you) in the next part of the ayah, showing who is indeed with them.
This is a basic preposition meaning 'with' or 'along with'
Added كُم at the end to show this means 'with you all' - used when talking to a group of people
This is a basic preposition that shows where something comes from or what it belongs to
The shadda (ّ) on م makes the sound stronger to add emphasis

steps.m: from/of (with emphasis, connecting to next word)
steps.n: The fatha (َ) at the end helps connect this word smoothly to the next word (ٱلْمُنتَظِرِينَ)

steps.m: to wait or watch for something
steps.n: This is the basic meaning of looking or waiting
Added vowels to connect the letters and make a past tense verb
Added اِنْتَ at the beginning to show doing the action with more focus and purpose - like waiting while really expecting something
Changed to describe someone who does the waiting
Added ين to make it plural and show it's affected by the word مِنَ (from) before it

steps.m: the ones who wait expectantly (after مِنَ)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and a final َ because it comes after مِنَ
This is a basic connecting word that shows sequence of events. It is a primitive particle with no root.

Added damma (ُ) and shadda with fatha (َّ) to complete the word. This word connects what comes after it with what came before, showing that one thing happens after another. Here it connects to the verb نُنَجِّى (we save), showing this saving happens after previous events.

steps.m: to rescue or deliver from danger

Added fatha at the end because this word is being acted upon by the verb نُنَجِّى (we save)
Added نَا at the end to show these are our messengers. This word is being acted upon by the verb نُنَجِّى (we save)

Adding ال makes it more specific, like saying 'the one who' instead of just 'who'

steps.m: to rescue or save someone from harm

Added vowels to connect the letters into a basic past-tense verb
Changed from a verb (to believe) to a noun meaning 'one who believes'. The م at the start and ؤ in the middle show it's about the person doing the action

steps.n: Changed to a command form by: 1) dropping the middle weak letter و, 2) adding damma on ق, and 3) adding sukun on ل. This is Allah commanding Prophet Muhammad ﷺ to speak to the people.

steps.m: in/within (connects شَكٍّ 'doubt' to what comes before)
steps.n: The ي at the end is written as ى when it's at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added اِ at start and ت after first letter to show the action happens to oneself
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start. This word parallels يَضِلُّ (he goes astray) in the ayah, showing the contrast between guidance and misguidance.
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to create the basic noun meaning 'self' or 'soul'
Added لِ at the beginning to show this is being done for or to something
Added ِ at the end to show this connects to the next word

steps.m: for himself (object of يَهْتَدِى - he is guided)
steps.n: Added هِۦ at the end to show this means 'his'. This word shows who benefits from the guidance mentioned in يَهْتَدِى (he is guided).
Added fatha to show this is a question word asking about who does something. This word is the subject of ضَلَّ (goes astray) in this ayah.
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
Connected the letters together with fatha marks to show this happened in the past

steps.m: he completely lost the right path (subject of فَإِنَّمَا)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shown by ّ) makes the meaning stronger. This word is connected to فَإِنَّمَا (then indeed) in this ayah, showing the result of someone's choice.
This is the base emphatic particle that adds certainty to what follows
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from what came before
Added مَا at the end which changes the meaning from emphasis to restriction (only/just)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense form
Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger - showing complete/intense straying
Added يَ at start to show present tense, changed middle vowel to kasra, and added damma at end to show he is doing the action
This is a preposition that shows something is physically or metaphorically above something else
The word changes form to prepare for adding a pronoun
Added ها which means 'her' or 'it' (feminine). This shows what is being gone astray upon in relation to يَضِلُّ (he goes astray).
Base form of the negative particle that negates 'I am a guardian over you'
Added long alif to emphasize the negation
Added وَ to connect this negation with the previous statement
This is the basic form of the first person pronoun with vowel marks added
Adding alif at the end makes it mean 'I'
The small alif (۠) above is added to help with pronunciation but doesn't change the meaning
This is a preposition derived from the concept of being above or elevated
The ى changes to يْ to prepare for adding a pronoun. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect meaning.
The suffix كُم means 'you all' and shows who the preposition affects. This word relates to أَنَا۠ (I am) in the ayah, showing what the speaker is not over/upon.
The root relates to the concept of trusting or depending on someone
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Changes from a verb (to take care of) to a noun meaning someone who takes care of things
Added بِ at beginning to show 'with/by'

steps.m: with/by a guardian (in the ayah: I am not over you as a guardian)
steps.n: Added tanween kasra to show indefiniteness and that it follows the preposition بِ
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he followed' to 'follow!' by adding alif at start and making it a command word. Like telling someone 'Follow this!'
The ت and ب merged together with a shadda, making the command stronger - like saying 'really follow!' instead of just 'follow!'

steps.m: and follow closely! (command to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ)
steps.n: Added و at start to connect with previous statement, and changed initial alif to hamza-wasl (ٱ)
This is a relative pronoun that connects the command 'follow' (وَٱتَّبِعْ) with what is being revealed. It works like 'what' in English when we say 'follow what I tell you.'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form basic past tense verb

steps.m: it is being revealed (to إِلَيْكَ - to you)
steps.n: Changed to present passive tense. The ى at the end is because this is a weak verb (ends with ي). This shows something is happening to someone - in this case, revelation is being given to the Prophet ﷺ.
The small alif (ٰ) is added above the ى to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
This is the basic preposition meaning 'to' or 'towards'. It's one of the most common prepositions in Arabic.
The ى changes to ي with sukun when we're going to add something to the end of the word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to you (referring to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ in يُوحَىٰٓ)
steps.n: Added the pronoun كَ (you) to show who the action is directed to. This كَ refers to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, who is being told to follow what is revealed to him.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Changed from 'he was patient' to 'be patient!' by adding اِ at start and changing vowels to make it a command to one person

steps.m: and be patient! (connected to وَٱتَّبِعْ - and follow)
steps.n: Added و to connect this command with the previous command 'follow' (وَٱتَّبِعْ)
This is the basic form of the word that shows a limit or endpoint. Since it's a particle, it doesn't come from a root.
Added shadda to strengthen the middle letter, which is common in particles.
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end to make it a complete particle meaning 'until'. This word connects to يَحْكُمَ (He judges) to show waiting until Allah makes His judgment.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added يَ at start to show it happens now, changed middle vowel to ُ and final vowel to ُ for present tense
Changed the final vowel to fatha to show it's dependent on something else
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding specific vowel patterns

This is the unique name for the One True God in Islam. The ال makes it definite (like 'the'), the shadda emphasizes the ل, and the ُ shows this is the subject of يَحْكُمَ (judges/decides) in this ayah.
Added vowel marks to show the correct pronunciation of the pronoun
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this word with what came before it
Adds the vowels 'fatha' and 'sukoon' to make the root into a noun meaning 'good'
Added damma at the end to show this word is possessed by الحٰكِمِين (the judges), forming the phrase 'best of judges'

steps.m: to judge wisely or make decisions

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed to show the person who does the judging by adding alif after first letter and kasra after second letter
Made plural to show many judges. This word is in the genitive case because it comes after خَيْرُ (best) in the phrase 'best of judges'
Added 'the' at the beginning to make it definite, and final fatha because it follows خَيْرُ (best of)
